This is why it is also called a **Metallograph**. ### Step-by-Step Image Formation 1. **Light Source:** A high-intensity **tungsten-halogen lamp** emits white light. 2. **Condenser & Aperture Diaphragm:** The light is collimated and focused onto the specimen surface. 3. **Objective Lens:** Collects the reflected light from the specimen (forms primary image). 4. **Eyepiece (Ocular):** Magnifies the primary image for the eye or camera. 5. **Camera (optional):** A digital CCD/CMOS sensor captures the microstructure for archival. > [!NOTE] > **Why can't we see atoms?** Because the **Abbe Diffraction Limit** of visible light is approximately: > $$d = \frac{\lambda}{2 \cdot NA} \approx \frac{550 \text{ nm}}{2 \times 0.95} \approx 290 \text{ nm}$$ > Optical microscopes cannot resolve features smaller than ~200 nm, which is why **Scanning Electron Microscopes (SEM)** are needed for higher magnifications. ## 2.3 Double Disc Polishing Machine — Working Principle The **Double Disc Polishing Machine** is the workhorse of any metallography lab. It consists of: - **Two counter-rotating horizontal discs** (typically 200–250 mm diameter) driven by an electric motor. - **Disc 1 (Left):** Used for **Coarse Grinding** (with water-cooled SiC emery papers). - **Disc 2 (Right):** Used for **Fine Polishing** (with diamond/alumina suspension on a cloth-covered disc). - A **water-spray system** continuously washes away debris and prevents heat build-up (which would otherwise alter the microstructure via phase transformations). ### Grinding Sequence (Emery Papers) | Stage | Grit Designation (Old / FEPA) | Particle Size (µm) | Purpose | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **1/0 or P120** | ~125 µm | Remove cutting damage, flatten | | 2 | **2/0 or P320** | ~46 µm | Remove prior scratches | | 3 | **3/0 or P600** | ~26 µm | Refine to fine scratches | | 4 | **4/0 or P1000** | ~18 µm | Pre-polish finish | | 5 | **Diamond/Alumina Paste (1 µm to 0.25 µm)** | 1.0 – 0.25 µm | Final **mirror polish** | > [!WARNING] > **Critical Rule:** Between each grit stage, the specimen must be **rotated by 90°**, and grinding continued until the *previous set of scratches* is completely obliterated. Failure to do this leaves "ghost scratches" that appear in the final microstructure as misleading artifacts. ## 2.4 Etching — The Art of Revealing the Invisible A perfectly polished surface under the microscope appears as a **featureless bright mirror** because all grains reflect light uniformly into the objective. **Etching** is the controlled chemical attack of this surface using a reagent that preferentially dissolves: - **Grain boundaries** (high-energy regions attacked first → appear as dark lines) - **Different phases** at different rates (e.g., ferrite vs. pearlite in mild steel) ### Standard Etchants (KTU Curriculum Table) | Material | Etchant Name | Composition | What It Reveals | Etching Time | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Mild Steel** | **Nital (2%)** | 2 mL HNO₃ + 98 mL Ethanol | Ferrite (light) + Pearlite (dark) grain boundaries | 5 – 10 seconds | | **Copper** | **Klemm's Reagent / Ammonium Persulfate** | (NH₄)₂S₂O₈ + NH₄OH | Annealing twins, grain boundaries | 30 – 60 seconds | | **Brass (Cu-Zn)** | **Ferric Chloride Reagent** | FeCl₃ + HCl + H₂O | α-phase (light) + β-phase (dark) | 10 – 30 seconds | | **Aluminium** | **Keller's Reagent** | 1 mL HF + 1.5 mL HCl + 2.5 mL HNO₃ + 95 mL H₂O | Grain boundaries, intermetallics | 10 – 20 seconds | > [!IMPORTANT] > Etching is done by **swabbing** (rubbing with cotton dipped in etchant) or by **immersion** (dipping the specimen face-down in a dish of etchant). **Over-etching** produces a blackened, burnt-looking surface; **under-etching** leaves the structure invisible. ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula / Relation | Symbol Explanation | Engineering Use | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Hall-Petch Equation:** $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y \cdot d^{-1/2}$ | $\sigma_y$ = yield stress, $d$ = mean grain diameter, $k_y$ = Hall-Petch coefficient | Predicts strength from grain size | | 2 | **Abbe Resolution Limit:** $d_{min} = \dfrac{0.61 \lambda}{NA}$ | $\lambda$ = wavelength, $NA$ = numerical aperture | Maximum useful magnification | | 3 | **Total Magnification:** $M_{total} = M_{obj} \times M_{eye}$ | $M_{obj}$ = objective, $M_{eye}$ = eyepiece magnification | Setting up the microscope | | 4 | **ASTM Grain Size Number:** $N = 2^{G-1}$ | $N$ = number of grains/in² at 100×, $G$ = ASTM number | Quantitative grain size reporting | | 5 | **Linear Intercept Method:** $\bar{L} = \dfrac{L_{total}}{N_{intercepted}}$ | $L_{total}$ = test line length, $N$ = grain boundaries crossed | Average grain size measurement | | 6 | **Numerical Aperture:** $NA = n \sin(\theta)$ | $n$ = refractive index, $\theta$ = half-angle of cone | Objective lens light-gathering | | 7 | **Area Fraction (Phase %):** $A_f = \dfrac{A_{phase}}{A_{total}} \times 100$ | Used in quantitative metallography | Estimate % pearlite, % ferrite, etc. | > [!NOTE] > **Note on KTU Exam:** For a 3-mark question, you may be asked to *state* the Hall-Petch equation and explain its significance. For 14-mark questions, derivation of grain-size-strength relationships is common. ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications - **Failure Analysis:** Investigating fractured aircraft, bridge, or boiler components to identify the root cause (e.g., grain boundary corrosion, overheating). - **Heat Treatment QC:** Verifying that a quenching/tempering operation has produced the desired phase (e.g., martensite in hardened steel). - **Casting & Forging QC:** Detecting shrinkage porosity, gas holes, or flow lines. - **Weld Inspection:** Examining the Heat-Affected Zone (HAZ) and weld fusion boundary. - **Material Certification:** Every batch of incoming raw material (e.g., aluminium sheets for aircraft) is microscopically examined as part of **AS9100 / ISO 9001** quality systems. # 3. Step-by-Step Experimental Procedure & Analytical Matrix > [!NOTE] > Since this is a **Laboratory Experiment** rather than a pure derivations chapter, Section 3 presents the **exhaustive step-by-step experimental protocol** (the laboratory "derivation" of microstructural data) along with the complete **Analytical Matrix** of expected microstructures for each material. ## 3.1 Aim of the Experiment (As per KTU 2024 Manual) **To prepare, polish, etch, and microscopically examine the microstructure of given engineering materials (Mild Steel / Copper / Brass / Aluminium), and to identify the phases and grain morphology.** ## 3.2 Apparatus Required (with Specifications) 1. **Optical / Metallurgical Microscope** — Magnification: 50X, 100X, 200X, 500X, 1000X; Built-in epi-illuminator. 2. **Double Disc Polishing Machine** — 200 mm discs, 0–1500 RPM, water cooling. 3. **Specimen Mounting Press** — Bakelite mounting (for small/irregular pieces). 4. **Emery Papers** — SiC, grades 1/0, 2/0, 3/0, 4/0. 5. **Polishing Cloths** — Velvet, Billiard, or synthetic suede. 6. **Diamond Paste / Alumina Suspension** — 6 µm, 3 µm, 1 µm, 0.25 µm. 7. **Etchants** — As per Section 2.4 table. 8. **Miscellaneous** — Beakers, tongs, cotton swabs, acetone for cleaning, hot air dryer, gloves, safety goggles. ## 3.3 Detailed Experimental Procedure (Step-by-Step) ### **Phase A — Specimen Preparation** 1. **Cutting:** Cut a small representative piece (~10–15 mm) from the bulk material using an abrasive cut-off wheel. The cut surface must be smaller than the final observed face. 2. **Mounting (Optional):** If the specimen is small/irregular, mount it in a **bakelite phenolic resin** using a mounting press at 140 °C and 2500 psi. 3. **Pre-grinding on a belt grinder (if needed):** Flatten the cut face using a 60-grit belt to remove the deep deformation layer. ### **Phase B — Grinding (on Disc 1 of the Double Disc Polisher)** 4. Place the **1/0 (P120) emery paper** on the left disc. Turn on the water spray. Hold the specimen firmly with the cut face down, *against* the rotation direction, with even pressure. 5. Grind until the surface is flat and the cutting marks are removed. Wash and dry the specimen. 6. Move to **2/0 (P320)** emery paper. **Rotate the specimen by 90°** from the previous scratch direction. Grind until all the 1/0 scratches are gone. 7. Repeat for **3/0 (P600)** and **4/0 (P1000)**. The final surface should show only fine, unidirectional, parallel scratches. > [!WARNING] > **PITFALL:** Students often apply *uneven pressure*, producing a *crowned* (convex) surface. Keep the specimen absolutely flat against the disc. ### **Phase C — Polishing (on Disc 2 of the Double Disc Polisher)** 8. Place a **polishing cloth** (velvet or suede) on the right disc. Apply a small quantity of **6 µm diamond paste** and a few drops of lubricant (DP-Lubricant or glycerine). 9. Polish the specimen, again rotated 90° from the final grinding direction. Polish until a *satin finish* is achieved. 10. Progress to **3 µm**, then **1 µm** diamond. For Aluminium, **0.25 µm diamond or colloidal silica** is used as the final step to prevent smearing. 11. The final surface must be a **perfect mirror** — reflecting the room light cleanly with no visible scratches. > [!NOTE] > Between each polishing stage, the specimen **must** be ultrasonically cleaned (or hand-washed with water and soap, then with acetone) to prevent **cross-contamination** of abrasive particles. ### **Phase D — Etching** 12. Clean the mirror-polished surface with acetone and dry with hot air. 13. Hold the specimen with tongs (NEVER with bare fingers — fingerprints contain corrosive salts). 14. **For Mild Steel (Nital 2%):** Immerse face-down in the etchant dish for **5–10 seconds**, or swab with cotton soaked in Nital for 10–20 seconds. 15. Immediately **rinse with running water**, then with **ethanol** (to prevent water staining), and dry with hot air. 16. **For Copper:** Etch in **Klemm's reagent** for 30–60 seconds; rinse and dry. 17. **For Brass:** Etch in **FeCl₃** reagent for 10–30 seconds; rinse and dry. 18. **For Aluminium:** Etch in **Keller's reagent** for 10–20 seconds; rinse and dry. > [!WARNING] > **CRITICAL SAFETY:** All etchants contain strong acids (HNO₃, HF, HCl). HF (Keller's reagent) is **extremely toxic** and requires **calcium gluconate gel** to be on hand. Always work in a **fume hood** with **gloves and goggles**. ### **Phase E — Microscopic Examination** 19. Place the etched specimen on the **microscope stage** with the etched face up. 20. Start with the **lowest magnification objective (5X or 10X)** to locate a representative area. 21. Switch to **higher magnifications (50X, 100X, 200X, 500X)** progressively. Refocus with the fine knob each time. 22. Adjust the **aperture diaphragm** to maximize contrast (closing it slightly sharpens grain boundaries). 23. Sketch the microstructure. Note: grain shape, color contrast, presence of twins, inclusions, and phases. 24. (If a camera is available) Capture a digital image and label it. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Expected Microstructures | Material | Phases Present | Typical Grain Morphology | Magnification Recommended | Key Features to Identify | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Mild Steel (e.g., AISI 1020)** | **Ferrite (α-Fe) + Pearlite** | Equiaxed ferrite grains (light) with **pearlite colonies** (dark, lamellar Fe + Fe₃C) at boundaries | 100X – 500X | Light ferrite matrix, dark pearlite islands; ~30–40% pearlite | | **Copper (pure, annealed)** | **Single-phase α-Cu (FCC)** | Equiaxed grains with prominent **annealing twins** (straight parallel bands across grains) | 100X – 200X | Many straight twin boundaries; clean grain interiors | | **Brass (e.g., 70/30 cartridge brass)** | **Single-phase α-brass (FCC)** | Equiaxed grains, occasional annealing twins, no second phase | 100X – 200X | Yellowish grain boundaries; possible rolling striations | | **Aluminium (commercially pure 1100)** | **Single-phase α-Al (FCC)** | Large equiaxed grains, no twins in annealed state | 50X – 200X | Very large, light grains; possible **FeAl₃ intermetallic inclusions** (dark specks) | ## 3.5 Calculation of Grain Size (Linear Intercept Method — Sample KTU Calculation) Given: A test line of total length $L_{total} = 5 \text{ cm} = 50 \text{ mm} = 50{,}000 \text{ µm}$ is drawn on a 100X micrograph of a mild steel specimen. The line crosses **40 grain boundaries**. **Step 1 — Mean Linear Intercept:** $$\bar{L} = \frac{L_{total}}{N_{boundaries}} = \frac{50{,}000 \, \mu m}{40} = 1250 \, \mu m = 1.25 \, mm$$ **Step 2 — Convert to True Length (Correction for 100×):** $$d_{true} = \frac{\bar{L}}{M} = \frac{1.25 \text{ mm}}{100} = 0.0125 \text{ mm} = 12.5 \, \mu m$$ **Step 3 — ASTM Grain Size Number ($G$):** Using the relation $N = 2^{G-1}$ where $N$ is the number of grains per square inch at 100X. For an average grain size of ~12.5 µm, $G \approx 9$ (fine grain). **Step 4 — Hall-Petch Strength Prediction (Demonstration):** For mild steel: $\sigma_0 = 70$ MPa, $k_y = 0.74$ MPa·m$^{1/2}$. $$\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y \cdot d^{-1/2} = 70 + 0.74 \times (12.5 \times 10^{-6})^{-0.5}$$ $$= 70 + 0.74 \times 282.8 = 70 + 209.3 = 279.3 \text{ MPa}$$ > [!NOTE] > This shows that *finer grain size directly increases the yield strength* of mild steel — the central engineering message of the entire experiment. ## 3.6 Result & Observation Table (Standard KTU Lab Record Format) | Sr. | Observation | Recorded Value / Sketch | |---|---|---| | 1 | Material identification | Mild Steel / Copper / Brass / Aluminium | | 2 | Initial specimen dimensions | ____ mm × ____ mm | | 3 | Mounting method used | Bakelite / No mounting | | 4 | Emery paper sequence used | 1/0 → 2/0 → 3/0 → 4/0 | | 5 | Polishing stages & abrasive | 6 µm → 3 µm → 1 µm diamond | | 6 | Etchant used | Nital 2% / Klemm's / FeCl₃ / Keller's | | 7 | Etching time | ____ seconds | | 8 | Microscope magnification used | 100X / 200X / 500X | | 9 | Phases observed | e.g., Ferrite + Pearlite | | 10 | Microstructure sketch | (attach labeled diagram) | | 11 | Average grain size | ____ µm | | 12 | ASTM Grain Size No. $G$ | ____ | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Metallographic Workflow > The Mermaid diagram below maps the **process flow, equipment interactions, and feedback loops** of a complete metallographic examination — the official "block schematic" presented in KTU lab manuals. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[START: Bulk Metal Sample] --> B[Sectioning
Abrasive Cut-off Saw] B --> C[Mounting
Bakelite Press at 140 C] C --> D[Coarse Grinding
Emery 1/0 on Disc 1] D --> E[Fine Grinding
Emery 2/0, 3/0, 4/0] E --> F[Check Scratch Direction
Rotate 90 deg] F --> G[Diamond Polishing
6, 3, 1 micron on Disc 2] G --> H{Mirror Finish
Achieved?} H -- No --> G H -- Yes --> I[Ultrasonic Cleaning
+ Acetone Rinse] I --> J[Etching
Nital / Klemm / FeCl3 / Keller] J --> K{Etching Time
Sufficient?} K -- Under-etched --> J K -- Over-etched --> L[Re-polish and Re-etch] K -- Correct --> M[Mount on Microscope Stage] M --> N[Low Mag Scan: 50X to 100X
Locate Representative Area] N --> O[High Mag Imaging: 200X to 500X] O --> P[Capture Digital Micrograph] P --> Q[Identify Phases + Grains] Q --> R[Measure Grain Size
Linear Intercept Method] R --> S[Apply Hall-Petch Relation] S --> T[Report: Grain Size, ASTM G,
Estimated Yield Strength] T --> U[END: Microstructure Analysis Complete] L --> G style A fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff style U fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff style D fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#000 style E fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#000 style G fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#000 style J fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#000 style M fill:#e1bee7,stroke:#000 style O fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#000 style S fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Optical Microscope — Light Path Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[Tungsten Halogen Lamp
Light Source] --> B1[Collector Lens] B1 --> C1[Aperture Diaphragm] C1 --> D1[Field Diaphragm] D1 --> E1[Vertical Illuminator
Half-Silvered Mirror] E1 --> F1[Objective Lens
NA 0.25 to 0.95] F1 --> G1[Specimen Surface
Etched Metal] G1 -->|Reflected Light| F1 F1 --> H1[Primary Image Plane] H1 --> I1[Eyepiece / Camera] I1 --> J1[Observer Eye OR
Digital CCD Sensor] J1 --> K1[Microstructure Image
Magnified 50X to 1000X] K1 --> K2[END: Visible Grain Structure] style A1 fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#000 style G1 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#000 style J1 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#000 style K1 fill:#b39ddb,stroke:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Double Disc Polishing Machine — Functional Schematic ```mermaid flowchart TB M1[1 HP Electric Motor] --> M2[Drive Shaft] M2 --> P1[Pulley A] M2 --> P3[Pulley B] P1 --> B1[Belt 1] P3 --> B3[Belt 2] B1 --> D1[LEFT DISC
Disc 1 - Coarse Grinding
Emery Papers] B3 --> D2[RIGHT DISC
Disc 2 - Fine Polishing
Diamond Paste + Cloth] W1[Water Inlet] --> S1[Water Spray Nozzle] S1 --> D1 S1 --> D2 S2[Coolant Drain Tray] --> D1 S2 --> D2 Spec1[Specimen Held By Hand] -->|Applied with even pressure| D1 Spec1 -->|Applied with even pressure| D2 style M1 fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#fff,color:#fff style D1 fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#000 style D2 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#000 style Spec1 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#000 style S1 fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#000 ``` ## 4.4 Microstructural Feature Identification Chart (for Sketches) ```mermaid flowchart TD Obs[Observed Microstructure] --> Q1{Is the metal
single-phase
or multi-phase?} Q1 -- Single phase
FCC metal --> SP1[Equiaxed grains
e.g., Aluminium, Copper, Brass] SP1 --> SP1A[Look for annealing twins:
STRAIGHT parallel lines inside grains] SP1A --> SP1B[Identify: PURE COPPER or BRASS] Q1 -- Multi-phase
Eutectoid reaction --> SP2[Light matrix + Dark islands] SP2 --> SP2A{Is the dark phase
lamellar or globular?} SP2A -- Lamellar --> SP2B[PEARLITE
Alternating Fe and Fe3C] SP2A -- Globular --> SP2C[Spheroidite] SP2B --> SP2D[Identify: MILD STEEL
Ferrite + Pearlite] Obs --> QC{Any defects
observed?} QC -- Yes --> QF[Quench Cracks /
Shrinkage Porosity /
Inclusions] QF --> QA[Report as Material Defect] style Obs fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#000 style SP1B fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#000 style SP2D fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#000 style QA fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#000 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** **Define the term "microstructure" of a metal. Why is metallographic examination important in materials engineering?** > **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > The **microstructure** of a metal is the internal structural arrangement of its constituent grains, phases, grain boundaries, and inclusions, observed at the microscopic level (typically 50X to 1000X magnification). > > **(1 mark)** — Definition as above. > **(1 mark)** — Microstructure governs all mechanical and physical properties: yield strength, ductility, hardness, toughness, electrical conductivity, and corrosion resistance. > **(1 mark)** — It serves as a quality-control tool to verify heat treatment, detect defects (cracks, porosity), and investigate failures in service. --- ### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **List the steps involved in the preparation of a metallographic specimen for micro-examination.** > **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > The standard six-step metallographic preparation sequence is: > 1. **Sectioning** — Cutting a representative sample using an abrasive cut-off wheel. > 2. **Mounting** — Embedding in bakelite for ease of handling and edge retention. > 3. **Grinding** — Sequential abrasion on emery papers (1/0 → 2/0 → 3/0 → 4/0). > 4. **Polishing** — Diamond/alumina paste on a rotating cloth disc to a mirror finish. > 5. **Etching** — Chemical attack with a suitable reagent (e.g., Nital for mild steel). > 6. **Microscopic Examination** — Observation and imaging under a metallurgical microscope. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > Per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, every Part B question has an **internal choice** — students attempt **ONE of the two** sub-questions. Below, two complete alternative questions are provided. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Modified]** **(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the working principle of a **metallurgical (optical) microscope**. Why is it called a "reflected-light" microscope? **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss the role of **etching** in revealing the microstructure. List suitable etchants for **(i) Mild Steel, (ii) Copper, (iii) Brass, (iv) Aluminium** with their compositions and typical etching times. **(7 marks)** #### **Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks:** > A **metallurgical microscope** is a reflected-light optical instrument used to examine opaque (non-transparent) metal specimens. **Working Principle (Step-by-Step, 5 marks):** 1. **Light Source:** A high-intensity tungsten-halogen lamp emits white light ($\lambda \approx 550$ nm). 2. **Condenser System:** The light passes through a **collector lens**, **aperture diaphragm** (controls intensity and contrast), and **field diaphragm** (controls illuminated area). 3. **Vertical Illuminator:** A **half-silvered mirror (or beam-splitter)** inside the microscope body redirects the light **vertically downward** through the objective lens onto the specimen. This is the defining feature of a metallurgical microscope. 4. **Objective Lens:** Focuses the light onto the etched specimen surface. 5. **Specimen Interaction:** Different phases and grain boundaries reflect light at *different intensities* (grain boundaries etched as grooves scatter light → appear dark). 6. **Reflected Light Path:** The reflected light re-enters the objective, passes back through the beam-splitter, and forms a **primary image** at the intermediate image plane. 7. **Eyepiece:** Magnifies the primary image (typically 10X) for the eye or digital camera. **Why "Reflected-Light" Microscope (2 marks):** Metals are **opaque** to visible light; light cannot pass *through* them as in biological microscopes. Therefore, the specimen must be illuminated from **above** and the image is formed from **reflected** (specular and scattered) light. Hence the name "metallographic reflected-light microscope." **Magnification Equation:** $$M_{total} = M_{objective} \times M_{eyepiece} = 50 \times 10 = 500\times$$ --- #### **Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks:** > **Role of Etching (3 marks):** > A perfectly polished metal surface is a *mirror* — all grains reflect light uniformly into the objective, producing a featureless bright image. **Etching** is the controlled chemical/electrochemical attack that selectively dissolves **grain boundaries** (which have higher energy) and **different phases** at different rates. This creates: > - **Topographical contrast** → grain boundaries appear as **dark lines**. > - **Phase contrast** → different phases etch to different depths and reflect light differently. > - **Color contrast** → in some etchants (Klemm's tint etch for copper), different orientations produce different colors. **Suitable Etchants (4 marks — 1 mark each):** | Material | Etchant | Composition | Etching Time | |---|---|---|---| | **(i) Mild Steel** | **Nital (2%)** | 2 mL HNO₃ + 98 mL Ethanol | 5 – 10 s | | **(ii) Copper** | **Klemm's Reagent** | 100 mL sat. Na₂S₂O₃ + 10 g K₂S₂O₅ | 30 – 60 s | | **(iii) Brass** | **Ferric Chloride (Kalling's)** | 5 g CuCl₂ + 100 mL HCl + 100 mL Ethanol | 10 – 30 s | | **(iv) Aluminium** | **Keller's Reagent** | 1 mL HF + 1.5 mL HCl + 2.5 mL HNO₃ + 95 mL H₂O | 10 – 20 s | --- ### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]** **(a)** Describe the **Hall-Petch relationship** and explain how grain size affects the **yield strength** of a metal. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A metallographic examination of **mild steel** reveals a mean linear intercept of **$\bar{L} = 18 \, \mu m$** at **100× magnification**. Determine: **(i)** the **ASTM grain size number $G$**, **(ii)** the **yield strength** using $\sigma_0 = 70$ MPa, $k_y = 0.74$ MPa·m$^{1/2}$, and **(iii)** the **% increase in yield strength** if the grain size is reduced to **5 µm** through thermomechanical processing. **(7 marks)** #### **Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks:** > The **Hall-Petch relationship** is the most important empirical equation in polycrystalline materials science. **(1 mark for statement)** **The Relation (1 mark):** $$\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y \cdot d^{-1/2}$$ **Where (1 mark):** - $\sigma_y$ = **yield strength** (MPa) - $\sigma_0$ = **friction stress** (resistance of the lattice itself, MPa) - $k_y$ = **Hall-Petch coefficient** (material constant, MPa·m$^{1/2}$) - $d$ = **mean grain diameter** (m) **Physical Mechanism (2 marks):** - Grain boundaries act as **obstacles to dislocation motion**. - Dislocations pile up at grain boundaries, creating a **back-stress**. - The stress required to propagate slip across the boundary is inversely proportional to $\sqrt{d}$. - **Finer grains → more boundaries → higher yield strength** (grain-boundary strengthening / *Hall-Petch strengthening*). **Engineering Significance (2 marks):** - The strongest structural steels (HSLA, dual-phase, martensitic) all exploit **fine grain sizes**. - **Thermo-Mechanical Controlled Processing (TMCP)** is specifically designed to refine grains. - Conversely, **overheating** during heat treatment causes grain coarsening, embrittling the steel. --- #### **Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks:** **Given:** $\bar{L} = 18 \, \mu m$ at 100X, $\sigma_0 = 70$ MPa, $k_y = 0.74$ MPa·m$^{1/2}$, final grain size $d_f = 5 \, \mu m$. **(i) ASTM Grain Size Number G (2 marks):** Number of grains per square inch at 100×: $$N \approx \left(\frac{1 \text{ inch}}{L}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{25{,}400 \, \mu m}{18 \, \mu m}\right)^2 = (1411)^2 = 1.99 \times 10^6 \text{ grains/in}^2$$ Using $N = 2^{G-1}$: $$1.99 \times 10^6 = 2^{G-1}$$ $$\log_2(1.99 \times 10^6) = G - 1 \Rightarrow G - 1 = 20.93 \Rightarrow G \approx 12$$ **(ii) Yield Strength for d = 18 µm (3 marks):** $$\sigma_y = 70 + 0.74 \times (18 \times 10^{-6})^{-0.5}$$ $$= 70 + 0.74 \times \frac{1}{\sqrt{18 \times 10^{-6}}}$$ $$= 70 + 0.74 \times 235.7 = 70 + 174.4 = 244.4 \text{ MPa}$$ **(iii) % Increase in Yield Strength (2 marks):** $$\sigma_{y,new} = 70 + 0.74 \times (5 \times 10^{-6})^{-0.5}$$ $$= 70 + 0.74 \times 447.2 = 70 + 331.0 = 401.0 \text{ MPa}$$ $$\% \text{ Increase} = \frac{401.0 - 244.4}{244.4} \times 100 = \frac{156.6}{244.4} \times 100 \approx 64.1\%$$ > **Engineering Conclusion:** Reducing the grain size from 18 µm to 5 µm increases the yield strength by **~64%**, demonstrating the extraordinary power of grain refinement in steel metallurgy. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Confusing "phase" with "grain"** — A *grain* is a single crystal of one phase; a *phase* is a region of uniform composition and structure. Mild steel has **2 phases** (ferrite, cementite) arranged in **2 microstructural constituents** (ferrite grains, pearlite colonies). > 2. **Writing "Nital 5%" or "Nital 10%"** — Standard etchant for mild steel is **2% Nital**. Stronger concentrations produce excessive attack and pitting. > 3. **Forgetting the 90° rotation rule** — Examiners specifically check whether students mention specimen rotation between emery papers. This is a **frequently tested 2-mark question**. > 4. **Wrong etching material for aluminium** — Do **NOT** use Nital on aluminium; it produces a thick, non-adherent oxide film. Use **Keller's reagent**. > 5. **Hall-Petch equation units** — $d$ must be in **metres (not µm)** when using SI units for $k_y$. A common 1-mark deduction. > 6. **Forgetting safety precautions for HF (Keller's reagent)** — A 1-mark deduction in viva questions. > 7. **Polishing too long** — Causes **"comet tails"** and **smearing** (especially in Al and Cu). Use minimum force and short intervals. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✨ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Must-Memorize Items for KTU ESE):** - ✅ **Microstructure** = Internal grain/phase arrangement visible under a microscope (50X – 1000X). - ✅ The **Six Steps** of metallography: **S**ectioning → **M**ounting → **G**rinding → **P**olishing → **E**tching → **E**xamination. - ✅ **Emery paper sequence:** **1/0 → 2/0 → 3/0 → 4/0** with **90° rotation** between each. - ✅ **Diamond paste sequence:** **6 µm → 3 µm → 1 µm → 0.25 µm** (final for Al). - ✅ **Nital (2%)** is for **Mild Steel**; **Keller's** for **Aluminium**; **FeCl₃** for **Brass**; **Klemm's** for **Copper**. - ✅ **Hall-Petch Equation:** $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y \cdot d^{-1/2}$ — **Finer grains = higher strength**. - ✅ **Optical microscope** uses **reflected (epi-) illumination** because metals are opaque. - ✅ **Abbe limit:** $d_{min} = 0.61\lambda / NA$ — sets the **maximum useful magnification** at ~1000X. - ✅ **Mild steel** microstructure = **Ferrite (light) + Pearlite (dark, lamellar)**. - ✅ **Copper / Brass** microstructure = equiaxed FCC grains with **annealing twins** (straight parallel bands). - ✅ **Aluminium** microstructure = large equiaxed grains, possible **FeAl₃ inclusions** (dark specks). - ✅ **ASTM Grain Size Number** $G$ relates to grains/in² at 100X by $N = 2^{G-1}$. - ✅ **Safety:** Always use **fume hood + gloves + goggles** when handling **Nital, Keller's, and Kalling's** reagents. - ✅ **Linear Intercept Method:** $\bar{L} = L_{total} / N_{boundaries}$ — most common grain size measurement. - ✅ **Examiner's favourite question:** "Why do we rotate the specimen 90° between emery papers?" — **Answer:** To ensure that the previous set of coarse scratches is *completely* removed by the finer paper, and to prevent carry-over contamination of abrasive particles. - ✅ **Units pitfall:** In Hall-Petch calculations, $d$ must be in **metres** (SI) when $k_y$ is in **MPa·m$^{1/2}$**.

topics.title: Fracture Surface Morphology of a Ductile or Brittle Material Using an Optical Microscope for Fractographic Characterisation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFL407 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS LABORATORY
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Fracture Surface Morphology of a Ductile or Brittle Material Using an Optical Microscope for Fractographic Characterisation**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Fractography** is the systematic study and analysis of fracture surfaces in materials to determine the fundamental causes, mechanisms, and origins of failure. As per KTU 2024 scheme laboratory standards (PCSFL407), this experiment involves the use of an **optical microscope (metallurgical microscope)** to characterise the surface morphology of fractured specimens, distinguishing between **ductile fracture** and **brittle fracture** signatures at magnifications typically ranging from **25X to 1000X**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 2):**
> The student must be able to identify and document characteristic fractographic features such as **dimples** (ductile), **cleavage facets and river patterns** (brittle), and the **fracture origin** using an optical microscope calibrated against standard reference graticules.

**Fracture Surface Morphology** refers to the geometric and topographical features visible on a broken surface — the "fingerprint" left behind by the failure process.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a road accident scene. A forensic investigator examines skid marks, broken glass patterns, and twisted metal to reconstruct what happened — speed, direction, point of impact. Similarly, a materials engineer examines a fracture surface like a **"metallurgical crime scene."**

- A **ductile material** (like chewing gum stretched slowly) leaves a **fibrous, matte, cup-and-cone surface** full of tiny craters called *dimples* — these are the "stretched-out" remains of microvoids that coalesced.
- A **brittle material** (like a dropped ceramic plate) leaves a **shiny, faceted, crystalline-looking surface** with sharp *cleavage planes* and *river patterns* — these are the "shatter" signatures of atomic bonds snapping without warning.

The **optical microscope** is our forensic magnifying glass. It uses reflected light (not transmitted, because fracture surfaces are opaque metals) to reveal these features.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric for KTU Lab Records:**
> The standard magnification for initial fracture survey is **50X to 100X**, while high-resolution identification of dimples vs. cleavage requires **400X to 1000X**. The **Numerical Aperture (NA)** of the objective lens governs resolution.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics (Bolded)

- **Resolving Power of Optical Microscope:** $\approx 0.2\ \mu m$ (limited by visible light wavelength $\lambda \approx 550\ nm$)
- **Standard Working Distance:** 8 mm – 15 mm
- **Illumination Modes:** Bright Field (BF), Dark Field (DF), Polarised Light
- **Typical Magnifications for Fractography:** 25X, 50X, 100X, 200X, 500X, 1000X

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Background — How Fracture Morphology Forms

The fracture surface morphology is the **direct physical record** of the micro-mechanism of crack propagation. To understand *why* a surface looks the way it does, we break down the failure into three logical stages:

### Stage 1: Crack Initiation
- **Ductile Materials:** Microvoids nucleate at inclusions, second-phase particles, or grain boundaries due to plastic incompatibility. Stress concentration at these sites is governed by $\sigma_{local} = 2\sigma_{applied}$ (for spherical inclusions).
- **Brittle Materials:** Crack initiates at pre-existing flaws (Griffith flaws), scratches, or at the elastic discontinuity at grain boundaries.

### Stage 2: Crack Propagation
- **Ductile:** Growth occurs by *microvoid coalescence* — voids grow, elongate, and link up. The fracture surface shows **dimples** (cup-like depressions).
- **Brittle:** Crack propagates by *cleavage* — atomic bonds break along specific crystallographic planes (e.g., {100} planes in BCC iron). The fracture surface shows **cleavage facets** with **river patterns** tracing the crack front direction.

### Stage 3: Final Fracture
- The cross-section reduces (necking in ductile) until sudden separation occurs. In ductile materials, this often forms a **cup-and-cone** fracture with the central fibrous zone and outer shear lip.

## 2.2 Identifying Features — A Diagnostic Table

| Feature Observed | Material Type | Mechanism | Diagnostic Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Microvoid coalescence (Dimples)** | Ductile | Plastic void growth | Overload failure, good energy absorption |
| **Cup-and-cone profile** | Ductile | Internal necking | Tensile ductile overload |
| **Cleavage facets** | Brittle | Transgranular cleavage | Low-temperature brittle failure |
| **River patterns** | Brittle | Step formation between cleavage planes | Crack propagation direction indicator |
| **Intergranular fracture** | Brittle | Grain boundary decohesion | Stress corrosion cracking, temper embrittlement |
| **Chevron marks** | Brittle | Macroscopic crack front tracking | Origin location pinpointing |
| **Shear lips** | Ductile | Final shear separation | Indicates ductility at edges |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Optical Microscopy & Fractography

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strict Formatting Note:** Vertical pipe characters used for absolute values or set notation are written as `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Formula | Description | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $M_{total} = M_{obj} \times M_{eyepiece}$ | Total magnification of compound microscope | dimensionless (X) |
| 2 | $d = \dfrac{0.61 \cdot \lambda}{NA}$ | Abbe diffraction limit (minimum resolvable distance) | $\mu m$ |
| 3 | $FOV = \dfrac{FOV_{eyepiece}}{M_{obj}}$ | Field of View on specimen surface | mm |
| 4 | $NA = n \cdot \sin(\theta)$ | Numerical Aperture of objective lens | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\sigma_f = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 E \gamma_s}{\pi a}}$ | Griffith criterion for brittle fracture (flaw size $a$) | MPa |
| 6 | $\% RA = \dfrac{A_f - A_i}{A_i} \times 100$ | Percentage Reduction in Area (ductility indicator) | % |
| 7 | $\% El = \dfrac{L_f - L_i}{L_i} \times 100$ | Percentage Elongation at fracture | % |
| 8 | $K_{Ic} = Y \sigma_c \sqrt{\pi a}$ | Fracture toughness (critical stress intensity factor) | $MPa\sqrt{m}$ |

Where:
- $\lambda$ = wavelength of light used (typically 550 nm for green)
- $n$ = refractive index of medium (air = 1.0, oil = 1.515)
- $\theta$ = half-angle of maximum cone of light entering lens
- $E$ = Young's Modulus
- $\gamma_s$ = Specific surface energy
- $a$ = half-length of internal flaw

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Fractography is **mandatory** in failure analysis of:
- **Aerospace components** (turbine discs, landing gear — brittle cleavage in cold weather = catastrophic)
- **Pressure vessels and pipelines** (identification of fatigue striations vs. overload)
- **Automotive crankshafts** (detecting fatigue origin at sub-surface inclusions)
- **Bridges and structural steel** (brittle fracture of older steel at low temperatures)

> [!NOTE]
> Optical microscopy gives **low-magnification overview**; for sub-micron dimple measurements, **SEM (Scanning Electron Microscopy)** is preferred. KTU 2024 lab syllabus focuses on optical microscopy for foundational understanding.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Total Magnification of a Compound Optical Microscope

The total magnification of a metallurgical microscope used in fractography is derived from the product of magnifications of the objective lens (which forms a real, inverted, magnified image) and the eyepiece (which magnifies this real image to form a virtual image at the eye's near point).

### Step-by-Step Mathematical Derivation

Consider an objective lens of focal length $f_o$ forming a real image at distance $v_o$ from the lens. The object distance is $u_o$.

The lateral magnification by the objective lens is:

$$m_o = \dfrac{v_o}{u_o}$$

When the final image is formed at the **least distance of distinct vision** $D = 250\ mm$ (standard convention), and the eyepiece acts as a simple magnifier, the angular magnification of the eyepiece is:

$$m_e = 1 + \dfrac{D}{f_e}$$

where $f_e$ is the focal length of the eyepiece. For a relaxed eye (image at infinity), this simplifies to:

$$m_e = \dfrac{D}{f_e}$$

The total magnification is the product:

$$M_{total} = m_o \times m_e$$

Using the thin lens equation for the objective, $\frac{1}{v_o} - \frac{1}{u_o} = \frac{1}{f_o}$, and assuming the object is placed just beyond the focal point (so $u_o \approx f_o$ for small specimens), we obtain:

$$M_{total} = \dfrac{v_o}{f_o} \times \dfrac{D}{f_e}$$

**Practical Example Calculation:**

A microscope uses a **10X objective** and a **10X eyepiece** with $f_e = 25\ mm$. Given $D = 250\ mm$:

$$M_{total} = 10 \times 10 = 100X$$

The field of view at this magnification, if the eyepiece FOV is 20 mm:

$$FOV_{specimen} = \dfrac{20\ mm}{10} = 2.0\ mm$$

This means at 100X, the visible fracture surface area is a **2 mm diameter circle** — adequate for observing cleavage facets and dimple zones.

## 3.2 Derivation: Abbe Resolution Limit (Why Optical Microscopy Has Limits)

The fundamental limit of optical microscopy (and therefore why we sometimes cannot resolve the finest dimples optically) was derived by **Ernst Abbe (1873)**. Two point sources can be resolved when the principal maximum of one Airy disk coincides with the first minimum of the other.

The minimum resolvable distance is:

$$d_{min} = \dfrac{\lambda}{2 \cdot NA}$$

However, with oblique illumination and a more rigorous treatment of diffraction:

$$d_{min} = \dfrac{0.61 \cdot \lambda}{NA}$$

where $\lambda$ is the wavelength of illuminating light and $NA = n \sin(\theta)$.

**Numerical Evaluation for a Typical Fractography Setup:**

For a typical 100X metallurgical objective:
- $NA = 1.25$ (oil immersion) or $0.95$ (dry)
- Using green light filter: $\lambda = 550\ nm = 0.55\ \mu m$

For **dry objective:**

$$d_{min} = \dfrac{0.61 \times 0.55}{0.95} = 0.353\ \mu m$$

For **oil immersion:**

$$d_{min} = \dfrac{0.61 \times 0.55}{1.25} = 0.268\ \mu m$$

This explains why dimples smaller than **0.3 µm** are difficult to characterise optically — they require SEM.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Decision Tree for Fracture Identification

The following sequential logic must be followed when examining a fracture surface optically:

**Step 1: Macroscopic Visual Examination (0X – 10X)**
- Examine overall profile: cup-and-cone? Flat? Angular?
- Note colour, oxidation, surface roughness.

**Step 2: Low-Power Optical Microscopy (25X – 50X)**
- Locate the **fracture origin** (often at the surface or near a defect).
- Identify major zones: fibrous zone, radial zone, shear lip.
- Look for chevron marks pointing back to the origin.

**Step 3: Mid-Power Optical Microscopy (100X – 200X)**
- Identify cleavage facets (brittle) — flat, shiny, crystallographic.
- Identify dimple regions (ductile) — fibrous, matte, micro-cratered.
- Map the transition zones.

**Step 4: High-Power Optical Microscopy (400X – 1000X)**
- Resolve individual dimple morphology — **equiaxed dimples** (central origin), **elongated parabolic dimples** (shear overload), **tear ridges** between dimples.
- Confirm cleavage steps and river patterns.

**Step 5: Photomicrography and Documentation**
- Capture images at 3 magnifications: **50X (overview), 200X (feature ID), 500X (fine detail)**.
- Calibrate scale bar using a stage micrometer.
- Record lighting mode (BF/DF) and objective NA.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Estimating Flaw Size from Griffith's Criterion

A brittle steel component has fractured. Given:
- $E = 200\ GPa$
- $\gamma_s = 8\ J/m^2$
- Applied stress at failure $\sigma_f = 350\ MPa$

Find the critical flaw size $2a$ using Griffith's equation:

$$\sigma_f = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 E \gamma_s}{\pi a}}$$

Rearranging for $a$:

$$a = \dfrac{2 E \gamma_s}{\pi \sigma_f^2}$$

Substituting values:

$$a = \dfrac{2 \times (200 \times 10^9) \times 8}{\pi \times (350 \times 10^6)^2}$$

Computing numerator:

$$2 \times 200 \times 10^9 \times 8 = 3.2 \times 10^{12}\ J/m$$

Computing denominator:

$$\pi \times (350 \times 10^6)^2 = \pi \times 1.225 \times 10^{17} = 3.848 \times 10^{17}$$

Therefore:

$$a = \dfrac{3.2 \times 10^{12}}{3.848 \times 10^{17}} = 8.32 \times 10^{-6}\ m = 8.32\ \mu m$$

Total flaw length:

$$2a = 16.64\ \mu m$$

This is a flaw that is **just visible** under high-power optical microscopy (~1000X) but is well within SEM resolution. This calculation reinforces the importance of fractographic examination.

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Inverted Metallurgical Microscope

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **functional interaction** of components during optical fractographic examination. Inverted metallurgical microscopes are preferred in fractography because bulky, irregular fracture specimens can be placed directly on the stage without slide preparation.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ILLUM["ILLUMINATION SUBSYSTEM"]
        L1["Halogen Lamp 6V 30W"] --> L2["Condenser Lens"]
        L2 --> L3["Aperture Diaphragm"]
        L3 --> L4["Field Diaphragm"]
        L4 --> L5["Specimen Stage with Fracture Sample"]
    end

    subgraph OPTICS["OPTICAL IMAGING SUBSYSTEM"]
        L5 --> O1["Objective Lens 5X 10X 20X 50X 100X"]
        O1 --> O2["Magnification Changer"]
        O2 --> O3["Beam Splitter"]
        O3 --> O4["Eyepiece 10X Trinocular Head"]
        O3 --> O5["Camera Port CCD Camera"]
    end

    subgraph CONTROL["CONTROL AND MEASUREMENT SUBSYSTEM"]
        O4 --> C1["Operator Eye Observation"]
        O5 --> C2["Digital Image Capture"]
        C2 --> C3["Image Analysis Software"]
        C3 --> C4["Scale Bar Calibration"]
        C4 --> C5["Fractographic Report Generation"]
    end

    subgraph SAMPLE["SPECIMEN HANDLING"]
        SH1["Fractured Specimen"] --> SH2["Ultrasonic Cleaning Acetone"]
        SH2 --> SH3["Drying and Mounting"]
        SH3 --> L5
    end

    C5 --> REPORT["Final Fracture Diagnosis Report"]
```

## 4.2 Procedural Flow Diagram — Fractography Experiment Workflow

This flowchart represents the **logical procedure** a KTU student must follow during the laboratory session, from specimen receipt to final report submission.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Step 1 Receive Fractured Specimen"] --> B["Step 2 Visual Macroscopic Inspection"]
    B --> C["Step 3 Clean Specimen with Acetone"]
    C --> D["Step 4 Mount on Inverted Microscope Stage"]
    D --> E["Step 5 Low Power 25X 50X Survey"]
    E --> F{"Step 6 Identify Fracture Origin?"}
    F -- Yes --> G["Step 7 Document Origin at 100X"]
    F -- No --> H["Step 7b Increase Magnification to 200X"]
    G --> I{"Step 8 Ductile or Brittle?"}
    H --> I
    I -- Ductile --> J["Step 9a Identify Dimples and Cup Cone"]

    K --> L
```

This diagram encapsulates the **diagnostic decision logic** that students must internalise for the KTU viva voce.

```mermaid
    Q2 --> Q4{"Microfeatures Visible?"}
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`
**Differentiate between ductile and brittle fracture surface morphology as observed under an optical microscope.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — full credit mapping):**

| Aspect | Ductile Fracture | Brittle Fracture |
|---|---|---|
| **Surface Appearance** | Matte, fibrous, dull | Shiny, granular, crystalline |
| **Profile Geometry** | Cup-and-cone with necking | Flat with little or no plastic deformation |
| **Microscopic Features** | Dimples (microvoids), tear ridges | Cleavage facets, river patterns, chevron marks |
| **Mechanism** | Microvoid nucleation, growth, and coalescence | Cleavage along crystallographic planes (e.g., {100} in BCC Fe) |
| **Energy Absorption** | High (significant plastic work) | Low (elastic energy release) |

*(1 mark for the table, 1 mark for mechanism distinction, 1 mark for at least one diagnostic micro-feature each)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`
**State and explain the Abbe resolution limit for an optical microscope. Why is this important for fractography?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The Abbe diffraction limit defines the **minimum resolvable distance** between two features under a light microscope:

$$d_{min} = \dfrac{0.61 \cdot \lambda}{NA}$$

where $\lambda$ is the wavelength of light (typically 550 nm for green filter) and $NA$ is the numerical aperture of the objective lens.

For a metallurgical microscope with 100X objective of NA = 0.95:

$$d_{min} = \dfrac{0.61 \times 0.55}{0.95} \approx 0.353\ \mu m$$

*(1 mark for formula, 1 mark for substitution, 1 mark for fractographic implication)*

**Importance in Fractography:** Dimples in highly ductile materials can be smaller than 1 µm, and cleavage step heights may be sub-micron. The Abbe limit dictates that **features smaller than ~0.3 µm cannot be resolved optically** — these require SEM. This defines the boundary of applicability of optical fractography in failure analysis.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students often *describe* ductile fracture features without specifying the **mechanism** (microvoid coalescence). Always link **appearance → mechanism → engineering consequence**.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Confusing *fibrous zone* (central, ductile, slow crack growth) with *shear lip* (outer, 45° slanted, final failure). These are distinct features and carry separate marks.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Not drawing a labelled sketch. In KTU valuation, a properly labelled **fracture surface diagram** with zones marked typically earns 2-3 marks even without text.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Using wrong units for fracture toughness ($MPa\sqrt{m}$, not $MPa/m^2$ or $N/mm^{3/2}$).
> - **Common Mistake 5:** Reporting the magnification without stating the **objective and eyepiece combination** used.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat labelled sketch, describe the **fracture surface morphology of a ductile material** as observed under an optical microscope. Discuss the formation of dimples and the cup-and-cone profile. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A specimen of mild steel fails under tension. The following data is obtained:
- Original diameter = 12 mm
- Final diameter at fracture = 8 mm
- Original gauge length = 50 mm
- Final gauge length = 65 mm

Calculate:
(i) Percentage reduction in area (% RA)
(ii) Percentage elongation (% El)
(iii) Comment on the ductility of the material based on fractographic examination. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Answer for Question A:

**(a) Ductile Fracture Surface Morphology (7 Marks):**

1. **Macroscopic Features (2 marks):** A ductile fracture in a cylindrical tensile specimen typically exhibits a **cup-and-cone fracture profile**. This consists of:
   - A central **fibrous zone** (rough, matte, perpendicular to tensile axis) — region of slow crack initiation and microvoid formation.
   - An outer **radial zone** — where the crack accelerates radially inward.
   - An outer **shear lip** at 45° to the tensile axis — final fast shear separation.

2. **Microscopic Features (3 marks):** Under optical microscopy at 200X–500X, the fibrous zone reveals:
   - **Equiaxed dimples** — small cup-shaped depressions formed by tensile microvoid coalescence.
   - **Elongated parabolic dimples** — found in shear lip regions, indicating shear-mode failure.
   - **Tear ridges** — sharp ridges of metal between adjacent dimples.
   - **Inclusions or second-phase particles** at the bottom of dimples — the nucleation sites.

3. **Formation Mechanism (2 marks):** Dimples form via three stages:
   - **Nucleation:** At inclusion/matrix interface due to plastic incompatibility.
   - **Growth:** Voids expand under triaxial tensile stress.
   - **Coalescence:** Adjacent voids link up by internal necking or shear, leaving a dimpled fracture surface.

*(Labelled sketch should include: fracture origin, fibrous zone, radial zone, shear lip, dimples — for 7 full marks)*

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- $D_i = 12\ mm$, $D_f = 8\ mm$
- $L_i = 50\ mm$, $L_f = 65\ mm$

**(i) Percentage Reduction in Area (2 marks):**

$$A_i = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times (12)^2 = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times 144 = 113.097\ mm^2$$

$$A_f = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times (8)^2 = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times 64 = 50.265\ mm^2$$

$$\% RA = \dfrac{A_i - A_f}{A_i} \times 100 = \dfrac{113.097 - 50.265}{113.097} \times 100$$

$$\% RA = \dfrac{62.832}{113.097} \times 100 = 55.55\%$$

**(ii) Percentage Elongation (2 marks):**

$$\% El = \dfrac{L_f - L_i}{L_i} \times 100 = \dfrac{65 - 50}{50} \times 100 = \dfrac{15}{50} \times 100 = 30\%$$

**(iii) Ductility Comment (3 marks):**

- A reduction in area of **55.55%** and elongation of **30%** indicate **highly ductile behaviour**.
- Under optical microscopy, the fracture surface should show **abundant dimples** across a wide fibrous zone, with prominent **shear lips** occupying 25-30% of the cross-section.
- The cup-and-cone profile should be clearly visible macroscopically.
- This level of ductility is typical of **hot-rolled low-carbon mild steel** in as-received (not cold-worked) condition.

**Verification of arithmetic:**
- $A_i = 113.097\ mm^2$ ✓
- $A_f = 50.265\ mm^2$ ✓
- $\% RA = 55.55\%$ ✓
- $\% El = 30\%$ ✓

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Describe the **fracture surface morphology of a brittle material** (e.g., grey cast iron or glass) as observed under an optical microscope. Explain cleavage facets, river patterns, and chevron marks. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **step-by-step procedure** for conducting fractographic examination of a failed tensile specimen using an inverted metallurgical microscope. Include specimen preparation, magnification selection, and documentation steps. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Answer for Question B:

**(a) Brittle Fracture Surface Morphology (7 Marks):**

1. **Macroscopic Appearance (2 marks):** Brittle fracture surfaces are typically:
   - **Flat and shiny** (little plastic deformation)
   - Oriented **perpendicular** to the maximum tensile stress
   - May show **chevron marks** — macroscopic V-shaped ridges pointing back toward the fracture origin.
   - No necking; cross-section at fracture $\approx$ original cross-section.

2. **Microscopic Features (3 marks):**
   - **Cleavage facets:** Flat, planar surfaces corresponding to specific low-index crystallographic planes (e.g., {100} in BCC iron). They appear as bright, smooth polygons under reflected light.
   - **River patterns:** A series of parallel steps on a cleavage facet that merge like tributaries of a river. They indicate the **local crack propagation direction** — the lines point away from the origin and converge in the direction of crack growth.
   - **Tongues:** Plate-shaped extensions protruding from cleavage facets, characteristic of BCC metals at low temperature.
   - **Grain boundary facets:** When the crack follows grain boundaries (intergranular), the surface shows a **rock-candy appearance** — irregular, polyhedral, with each face being a former grain boundary.

3. **Mechanism — Transgranular Cleavage (2 marks):**
   - The crack propagates by successive breaking of atomic bonds along cleavage planes.
   - When the crack crosses from one grain to another at different orientation, the cleavage planes shift, creating steps that merge into **rivers**.
   - The density and direction of river patterns are diagnostic of crack velocity and origin.

*(Labelled sketch must include: cleavage facets, river patterns, chevron marks, fracture origin indication)*

**(b) Procedure for Optical Fractographic Examination (7 Marks):**

| Step | Action | Purpose / Marks |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Specimen receipt and documentation** — record specimen ID, material, loading history, photograph as-received | 1 mark |
| 2 | **Macroscopic visual inspection** — note colour, oxidation, fracture profile, location of obvious defects | 1 mark |
| 3 | **Specimen cleaning** — ultrasonic cleaning in acetone for 5-10 minutes to remove debris, oil, and corrosion products | 1 mark |
| 4 | **Sectioning (if needed)** — for large specimens, cut a smaller portion containing the fracture surface using a slow-speed saw with coolant | 0.5 mark |
| 5 | **Mounting on inverted microscope stage** — place directly with fracture surface facing down toward objective | 0.5 mark |
| 6 | **Initial low-power survey (25X–50X)** — scan entire surface to locate fracture origin and identify zones (fibrous, radial, shear lip) | 1 mark |
| 7 | **Mid-power examination (100X–200X)** — identify dimples vs. cleavage facets; map micro-feature distribution | 1 mark |
| 8 | **High-power examination (400X–500X)** — resolve individual dimple or facet morphology; record tear ridges, river patterns | 0.5 mark |
| 9 | **Photomicrography** — capture images at 3 standard magnifications (50X, 200X, 500X) with **scale bar calibration** using a stage micrometer | 0.5 mark |

**Total: 7 marks** distributed as shown.

**Final Documentation:**
- Prepare a **lab report** with: (i) specimen details, (ii) macroscopic observations with sketch, (iii) photomicrographs with scale bars, (iv) feature identification table, (v) conclusion on fracture mode (ductile/brittle/mixed).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

- ✅ **Fractography** = study of fracture surfaces to determine failure cause and mechanism.

- ✅ **Ductile fracture signs:** Dimples (microvoids), cup-and-cone profile, fibrous matte surface, shear lips.

- ✅ **Brittle fracture signs:** Cleavage facets, river patterns, chevron marks, shiny flat surface, no necking.

- ✅ **Mechanisms:**
  - Ductile → Nucleation + Growth + Coalescence of microvoids
  - Brittle → Transgranular cleavage (along {100} BCC, {111} FCC) or Intergranular decohesion

- ✅ **Optical Microscope Resolution:** $d_{min} = \dfrac{0.61 \lambda}{NA}$, typically ~0.3 µm — features smaller than this need SEM.

- ✅ **Total Magnification:** $M = M_{obj} \times M_{eyepiece}$. Standard fractography uses 50X, 200X, 500X.

- ✅ **Standard specimen prep:** Ultrasonic cleaning in acetone; NO polishing (would destroy fracture surface).

- ✅ **Always record:** Objective NA, illumination mode (BF/DF), magnification, scale bar.

- ✅ **Field of View:** $FOV_{specimen} = \dfrac{FOV_{eyepiece}}{M_{obj}}$ — smaller at higher magnification.

- ✅ **Ductility indicators:** % Elongation > 10% and % RA > 20% → ductile; both < 5% → brittle.

- ✅ **Fracture origin location:** Found by tracing **chevron marks** (macroscopic) or **river patterns** (microscopic) backward to their convergence point.

- ✅ **Always link** observation → mechanism → engineering consequence in viva answers.

- ✅ **Lab report essentials:** Photomicrograph + scale bar + feature labels + conclusion on failure mode.

- ✅ **Typical mild steel ductile failure:** % RA ~ 50-60%, % El ~ 25-35%, large fibrous zone with dimples.

- ✅ **Typical grey cast iron brittle failure:** % RA ~ 0%, % El ~ 0.5%, cleavage facets visible in ferrite matrix with graphite flakes acting as stress concentrators.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fracture Toughness of a Material Using a Universal Testing Machine (UTM)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFL407 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS LABORATORY
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Fracture Toughness of a Material Using a Universal Testing Machine (UTM)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Fracture Toughness** is a quantitative measure of a material's resistance to brittle fracture when a crack is present. Formally, it is defined as the **critical value of the stress intensity factor (K)** at which a pre-existing sharp crack in a structure begins to propagate unstably under Mode I (tensile opening) loading. The plane-strain fracture toughness is denoted as **K_Ic** and is expressed in units of **MPa·√m**.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (ASTM E399 Standard):**  
> "Plane-strain fracture toughness, K_Ic, is the crack-tip-stress-intensity factor at which a crack in a linearly-elastic body, constrained to deform under plane-strain conditions, begins to grow rapidly."

In the context of the **Universal Testing Machine (UTM)**, fracture toughness is determined by monotonically loading a notched, fatigue pre-cracked specimen (typically a **Single Edge Notched Bend (SENB)** specimen per ASTM E399 / IS 15964) and recording the **Load (P) vs. Crack Opening Displacement (COD)** curve until fracture.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Zipper Tab" of a Material

Imagine a sheet of paper with a small cut on its edge. If you try to tear it, you instinctively start pulling the cut — because the **stress at the tip of the cut is amplified**, much higher than the average stress on the sheet. The cut is the **crack tip**, and the energy required to extend that cut depends on the material.

Now, picture two sheets:
- **Sheet A:** Thin cheap paper → tears instantly → low fracture toughness.
- **Sheet B:** A thick laminated card with woven fibers → you must pull hard before the cut extends → high fracture toughness.

The **UTM fracture toughness test** is a scientific way to *quantify* how much force per unit crack length a material can tolerate before the "zipper" runs catastrophically across the specimen. The **COD gauge** acts like a precise digital caliper that records the gap at the crack mouth as it opens.

## 1.3 Why Fracture Toughness Cannot Be Replaced by Tensile Strength

| Property | What It Measures | Limitation |
|----------|------------------|------------|
| **Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS)** | Maximum stress a smooth specimen can carry | Ignores cracks / flaws |
| **Impact Energy (Charpy / Izod)** | Energy absorbed in a sudden impact | Qualitative, geometry-dependent |
| **Fracture Toughness (K_Ic)** | Critical stress field at a sharp crack tip | Quantitative design parameter |

> [!NOTE]  
> **Design Relevance:** K_Ic is a *direct input* in damage-tolerant design of pressure vessels, aircraft fuselages, bridges, and offshore platforms. A material with high UTS but low K_Ic can still fail catastrophically because of microscopic flaws (e.g., high-strength steels vs. mild steel).

## 1.4 Standard Specimens Used in UTM Testing

Per **ASTM E399 / IS 15964**, two specimen geometries are permitted in UTM fracture tests:
1. **SENB (Single Edge Notched Bend)** — three-point bend test, also called the *Chevron-Notched* or *Straight-Notched Bend* test.
2. **CT (Compact Tension)** — compact tension specimen, also loadable on a UTM with clevis grips.

For an undergraduate KTU lab, the **SENB (Three-Point Bend)** configuration is by far the most common, because it requires only a standard three-point bend fixture, which is a routine accessory of the UTM.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Theory: Linear Elastic Fracture Mechanics (LEFM)

Fracture toughness testing rests on **LEFM**, which is valid only when the plastic zone at the crack tip is small compared to:
- The specimen thickness **B**
- The crack length **a**
- The unbroken ligament **(W − a)**

These validity requirements yield the famous plane-strain thickness criterion:
$$ B, \ a, \ (W - a) \ \geq \ 2.5 \left( \frac{K_{Ic}}{\sigma_{Y}} \right)^{2} $$

where $\sigma_{Y}$ is the **0.2 % offset yield strength** of the material.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Plane-strain condition** ensures tri-axial constraint at the crack tip, giving the *lowest* (worst-case) fracture toughness. If the specimen is too thin, the result is called **K_c** (apparent toughness), which is *not* a true material property.

## 2.2 The Stress Intensity Factor (K) Concept

For a through-thickness crack of length **a** in an infinite plate loaded remotely by stress **σ**, the **Mode I stress field** near the crack tip is:

$$ K_{I} = \sigma \sqrt{\pi a} \quad \text{(for an infinite plate)} $$

The stress field components are:
$$ \sigma_{ij} = \frac{K_{I}}{\sqrt{2\pi r}} \cdot f_{ij}(\theta) $$

This is **singular** (goes to infinity) as r → 0, meaning real materials cannot sustain it indefinitely; they yield plastically in a small zone near the tip. The **fracture criterion** is then:

$$ K_{I} \ \geq \ K_{Ic} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Catastrophic crack extension} $$

For a finite-size specimen, the stress intensity factor depends on the **geometry factor** $f\!\left(\tfrac{a}{W}\right)$ and is proportional to the **applied load P**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table collects every formula required for the UTM fracture-toughness experiment. All variables and units are KTU-board-exact.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units |
|---|----------|---------|-------|
| 1 | Stress Intensity Factor (SENB) | $K_{Q} = \dfrac{P_{Q} \cdot S}{B \cdot W^{3/2}} \cdot f\!\left(\dfrac{a}{W}\right)$ | $\text{MPa}\sqrt{\text{m}}$ |
| 2 | Geometry factor $f(a/W)$ (SENB) | $f = \dfrac{3\sqrt{a/W}}{2\,(1 + 2a/W)\,(1 - a/W)^{3/2}} \left[ 1.99 - \dfrac{a}{W}\!\left(1 - \dfrac{a}{W}\right)\!\left(2.15 - 3.93 \dfrac{a}{W} + 2.7 \left(\dfrac{a}{W}\right)^{2}\right) \right]$ | dimensionless |
| 3 | Critical load (5 % secant) | $P_{Q} = \dfrac{P_{\max}\bigl(\text{or }P_{5\%}\bigr)}{\text{per } P_{\max}/P_{Q} \le 1.10 \text{ rule}}$ | N or kN |
| 4 | Plane-strain validity (thickness) | $B \ge 2.5 \left( \dfrac{K_{Ic}}{\sigma_{Y}} \right)^{2}$ | m |
| 5 | Plastic-zone size estimate | $r_{p} = \dfrac{1}{6\pi} \left( \dfrac{K_{I}}{\sigma_{Y}} \right)^{2}$ (plane stress) ; $r_{p} = \dfrac{1}{3\pi} \left( \dfrac{K_{I}}{\sigma_{Y}} \right)^{2}$ (plane strain, Dugdale) | m |
| 6 | Effective crack length (from COD) | $a_{e} = W \cdot \left[ 0.999748 - 3.9504 u + 2.9821 u^{2} - 3.21408 u^{3} + 51.51664 u^{4} - 113.031 u^{5} \right]$ ; $u = \dfrac{1}{S} \sqrt{\dfrac{E \cdot B \cdot v}{P}}$ (compliance) | m |
| 7 | Conditional K_Ic = K_Q if valid | If $P_{\max}/P_{Q} \le 1.10$ and thickness valid, then $K_{Q} \equiv K_{Ic}$ | $\text{MPa}\sqrt{\text{m}}$ |

> [!NOTE]  
> **Notation used in KTU labs:**  
> $B$ = specimen thickness, $W$ = specimen depth, $a$ = total crack length (notch + fatigue pre-crack), $S$ = support span, $P$ = applied load, $v$ = crack-mouth opening displacement (CMOD) read by the COD gauge, $E$ = Young's modulus.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Typical K_Ic range | Why It Matters |
|----------|--------------------|----------------|
| **Aerospace (Al 7075-T6)** | ~30 MPa√m | Damage-tolerant airframe design, fuselage skin |
| **Shipbuilding (DH-36 steel)** | ~80–150 MPa√m | Brittle-fracture arrest in arctic-grade hulls |
| **Pipeline (API 5L X65)** | ~150–220 MPa√m | Crack-arrestors in gas transmission |
| **Ceramics (Alumina)** | ~3–5 MPa√m | Catastrophic failure prevention in cutting tools |
| **Toughened polymers (PC)** | ~2–3 MPa√m | Failsafe design of helmets, transparent shields |

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> The KTU lab UTM test is essentially a *miniature* version of the same procedure used to qualify these materials before they enter service.

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Geometry Factor $f(a/W)$ for the SENB Specimen

The stress intensity factor for a three-point bend specimen with span $S$, depth $W$, thickness $B$, and crack length $a$ is obtained by integrating the influence-function (Green's function) of a single edge crack under a point load. The result, first given by **Srawley and Gross (1976)**, is the expression tabulated earlier. The complete derivation is as follows.

### Step 1 — Define the normalized crack length

$$ \alpha = \frac{a}{W} \quad ; \quad 0.45 \le \alpha \le 0.55 \ \text{(recommended for ASTM E399)} $$

This range keeps the geometry factor insensitive to small machining errors.

### Step 2 — Start from the LEFM energy-release-rate form

For a single-edge-notched bend specimen, the **compliance** $C = v/P$ (ratio of COD to load) is given by the boundary-collocation solution of Gross and Srawley:

$$ E \cdot B \cdot \frac{v}{P} = \left[ 6 \left(\frac{S}{W}\right)^{2} \cdot V_{1}(\alpha) \right] $$

where $V_{1}(\alpha)$ is the **dimensionless crack-mouth opening compliance** function. The stress intensity factor is recovered by differentiating the compliance with respect to crack length:

$$ K_{I} = \sqrt{ \frac{P^{2}}{2B} \cdot \frac{\partial C}{\partial a} \cdot E } $$

### Step 3 — Carry out the differentiation

After substantial algebra (and fitting the result to a 5 % secant by ASTM), the differentiation produces:

$$ \frac{\partial C}{\partial a} \ \Rightarrow \ f(\alpha) = \frac{3 \sqrt{\alpha}}{2 (1 + 2\alpha)(1 - \alpha)^{3/2}} \left[ 1.99 - \alpha(1 - \alpha)\left( 2.15 - 3.93\alpha + 2.7 \alpha^{2} \right) \right] $$

Multiplying by the pre-factor $\dfrac{PS}{BW^{3/2}}$ gives the **complete K expression**:

$$ K_{I} = \frac{P S}{B W^{3/2}} \cdot \frac{3\sqrt{\alpha}}{2(1 + 2\alpha)(1 - \alpha)^{3/2}} \left[ 1.99 - \alpha(1 - \alpha)\left( 2.15 - 3.93\alpha + 2.7 \alpha^{2} \right) \right] $$

### Step 4 — Verify the limit as $a/W \to 0$

When $\alpha \to 0$ (no crack), the geometry factor must tend to zero (no singular stress field). Inspection shows the leading $\sqrt{\alpha}$ term forces $f \to 0$ as required.

### Step 5 — Evaluate at $a/W = 0.5$ (the recommended mid-range)

Substituting $\alpha = 0.5$:

$$ \sqrt{\alpha} = 0.7071 $$
$$ (1 + 2\alpha) = 2.0 $$
$$ (1 - \alpha)^{3/2} = 0.5^{1.5} = 0.3536 $$

The leading pre-factor evaluates to:

$$ \frac{3 \times 0.7071}{2 \times 2.0 \times 0.3536} = \frac{2.1213}{1.4142} = 1.500 $$

The bracket term:

$$ 1.99 - 0.5 \times 0.5 \times (2.15 - 3.93 \times 0.5 + 2.7 \times 0.25) = 1.99 - 0.25 \times (2.15 - 1.965 + 0.675) = 1.99 - 0.25 \times 0.860 = 1.99 - 0.215 = 1.775 $$

So:

$$ f(0.5) = 1.500 \times 1.775 = 2.663 $$

> [!NOTE]  
> The value $f(0.5) \approx 2.66$ is the value KTU students should *memorize* — it allows a quick on-the-spot check that the test data is reasonable.

Hence the final K expression at $a = W/2$ reduces to:

$$ K_{Ic} \approx 2.66 \times \frac{P_{Q} S}{B W^{3/2}} \quad \text{(for a/W = 0.5)} $$

## 3.2 Derivation of the 5 % Secant Critical Load $P_Q$

The 5 % secant rule (per ASTM E399 §9.1.1) is used to extract a *single* representative load from the (often non-linear) P–v record.

### Step 1 — Draw the initial compliance slope

On the P–v record, draw a straight line $OL$ through the origin along the **initial linear elastic portion** of the curve.

### Step 2 — Construct the secant

On the load axis, mark the point corresponding to a load that is **5 % less** than the secant would predict at the same v, i.e.:

$$ \text{Slope of secant} = 0.95 \times (\text{slope of initial elastic line}) $$

### Step 3 — Define $P_Q$

The intersection of this 95 %-slope secant with the experimental P–v curve is the conditional load $P_Q$.

### Step 4 — Check the $P_{\max} / P_Q$ ratio

If $\dfrac{P_{\max}}{P_Q} \le 1.10$, the test is **valid** and $K_Q = K_{Ic}$.
If $\dfrac{P_{\max}}{P_Q} > 1.10$, the test result is **invalid**; the specimen has experienced too much plasticity. A larger specimen or a more brittle material must be used.

Mathematically:

$$ P_{Q} = P \Big|_{v = v_{0}} \quad \text{where} \quad v_{0} = \frac{P_{Q}}{0.95 \, C_{i}} ; \quad C_{i} = \left. \frac{dv}{dP} \right|_{\text{initial}} $$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Given:** An SENB specimen is tested on the UTM.
- Specimen thickness: $B = 20\ \text{mm} = 0.020\ \text{m}$
- Specimen depth: $W = 40\ \text{mm} = 0.040\ \text{m}$
- Crack length: $a = 20\ \text{mm} = 0.020\ \text{m}$
- Span: $S = 160\ \text{mm} = 0.160\ \text{m}$
- Yield strength: $\sigma_{Y} = 350\ \text{MPa}$
- Critical load (5 % secant): $P_{Q} = 18\ \text{kN} = 18{,}000\ \text{N}$
- Maximum load: $P_{\max} = 19.5\ \text{kN}$

**Step 1 — Compute $a/W$**

$$ \alpha = \frac{a}{W} = \frac{0.020}{0.040} = 0.50 $$

**Step 2 — Look up / compute the geometry factor**

From the worked derivation above: $f(0.5) = 2.663$.

**Step 3 — Compute $K_Q$**

$$ K_{Q} = \frac{P_{Q}\,S}{B\,W^{3/2}} \cdot f\!\left(\frac{a}{W}\right) = \frac{18{,}000 \times 0.160}{0.020 \times (0.040)^{1.5}} \times 2.663 $$

Denominator:

$$ B \cdot W^{1.5} = 0.020 \times (0.040)^{1.5} = 0.020 \times 0.008 \, \text{m}^{1.5} = 1.6 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{m}^{2.5} $$

Numerator:

$$ P_{Q} \cdot S = 18{,}000 \times 0.160 = 2{,}880 \ \text{N}\cdot\text{m} $$

Base term:

$$ \frac{2{,}880}{1.6 \times 10^{-4}} = 1.8 \times 10^{7} \ \text{N/m}^{1.5} = 18.0 \ \text{MPa}\sqrt{\text{m}} $$

Multiplying by $f = 2.663$:

$$ K_{Q} = 18.0 \times 2.663 = 47.93 \ \text{MPa}\sqrt{\text{m}} $$

**Step 4 — Check the $P_{\max}/P_Q$ criterion**

$$ \frac{P_{\max}}{P_{Q}} = \frac{19.5}{18.0} = 1.083 \le 1.10 \quad \checkmark \ \text{Valid} $$

**Step 5 — Check the plane-strain thickness criterion**

$$ B_{\text{required}} = 2.5 \left( \frac{K_{Ic}}{\sigma_{Y}} \right)^{2} = 2.5 \left( \frac{47.93}{350} \right)^{2} = 2.5 \times (0.1370)^{2} = 2.5 \times 0.01876 = 0.0469\ \text{m} = 46.9\ \text{mm} $$

But the actual $B = 20\ \text{mm} < 46.9\ \text{mm}$ → **The specimen is too thin to satisfy plane-strain conditions.**

> [!WARNING]  
> **The KTU board examiner will deduct 2 marks here** if you report a numerical $K_{Ic}$ without this thickness check. You must report the result as $K_{Q}$ (conditional) and state that the test is *not* a valid $K_{Ic}$ measurement; the actual value would be obtained by repeating with a thicker specimen.

**Step 6 — Estimate the plastic-zone size (informative)**

$$ r_{p} = \frac{1}{3\pi}\left(\frac{K_{Q}}{\sigma_{Y}}\right)^{2} = \frac{0.1370^{2}}{3\pi} = \frac{0.01876}{9.4248} = 0.00199\ \text{m} \approx 2\ \text{mm} $$

Since $r_{p} \ll a, B, (W-a)$ the LEFM assumption is *locally* satisfied, but the bulk constraint is not.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

The fracture-toughness experiment cannot be drawn faithfully as a flow chart alone — both a **block-level functional flow** of the test procedure and a **schematic of the SENB specimen + UTM loading** are useful. Per the protocol, the physical loading schematic is rendered as a Mermaid block diagram of the functional architecture, and the UTM loading rig is described textually with a callout.

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of the UTM Fracture-Toughness Test

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Specimen Preparation: Cut SENB from stock] --> B[Notch Milling: EDM or slitting saw]
    B --> C[Fatigue Pre-cracking on Resonance or Servo-hydraulic machine]
    C --> D[Pre-crack length measured optically a is between 0.45W and 0.55W]
    D --> E[Mounting in UTM Three-Point Bend Fixture]
    E --> F[Attach COD Gauge at Crack Mouth]
    F --> G[Set UTM Crosshead Speed 0.5 to 1.0 mm per minute]
    G --> H[Acquire P versus v Record Until Fracture]
    H --> I[Draw Initial Compliance Line OL]
    I --> J[Construct 95 percent Secant]
    J --> K{Does secant intersect curve before Pmax}
    K -- Yes --> L[Read PQ from Intersection]
    K -- No --> M[Report PQ equals Pmax and flag as invalid]
    L --> N[Compute alpha equals a over W]
    N --> O[Evaluate Geometry Factor f of alpha]
    O --> P[KQ equals PQ S over B W to the 1.5 times f of alpha]
    P --> Q{Pmax over PQ less than or equal to 1.10}
    Q -- Yes --> R[Candidate K Ic equals KQ]
    Q -- No --> S[Invalid: Use Larger Specimen]
    R --> T{B greater than or equal to 2.5 K Ic over sigmaY squared}
    T -- Yes --> U[Final Result: K Ic in MPa square root m]
    T -- No --> V[Report as Conditional KQ Only]
```

## 4.2 Schematic Description of the UTM Loading Rig

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> Because Mermaid cannot reliably render physical 3-D rigs, the test rig is described layer-by-layer. In the KTU lab record, sketch this layout on the left side of the page before the data table.

**Top to bottom along the loading axis:**

1. **Upper crosshead** of the UTM (moving platen).
2. **Loading nose (roller)** — single-point contact, sits above the specimen centre line.
3. **SENB specimen** — rests horizontally on the lower fixture; the **machined notch + fatigue crack** faces **downward** in a standard bend test, so the crack is in tension (Mode I).
4. **Two support rollers** — at span $S = 4W$ per ASTM E399 recommendation. The specimen must overhang each support by at least $W/2$ to avoid end-bending effects.
5. **COD (Crack Opening Displacement) gauge** — clipped to the specimen face using two knife-edge supports straddling the notch mouth; outputs an analog voltage proportional to $v$, fed to the UTM's auxiliary channel.
6. **Load cell** — the UTM's standard load cell between the upper platen and the loading nose; outputs $P$.

**Key callouts on the sketch:**

| Callout | Where to Mark on the Sketch | Value to Record |
|---------|------------------------------|-----------------|
| (i) Span $S$ | Between the two support roller centres | mm |
| (ii) Crack length $a$ | Notch + pre-crack, measured on both faces | mm |
| (iii) Specimen thickness $B$ | Side view of the specimen | mm |
| (iv) Specimen depth $W$ | Front view of the specimen | mm |
| (v) COD gauge output | Right edge of the sketch, arrow into the data table | mm |
| (vi) Load cell output | Top edge of the sketch, arrow into the data table | N |

## 4.3 Typical P–v Curve Topology

| Curve Shape | Meaning | Validity |
|-------------|---------|----------|
| **Linear-elastic + pop-in** | Brittle material; sudden crack jump | Often valid; $P_Q$ from 5 % secant before pop-in |
| **Linear-elastic + small non-linearity** | Mildly ductile | Use 5 % secant; check $P_{\max}/P_Q$ |
| **Large non-linearity (plastic hinging)** | Ductile material, e.g. mild steel at RT | Invalid for $K_{Ic}$; use J-integral (ASTM E1820) |
| **Pop-in then stable growth** | Mixed | Use the pop-in load as $P_Q$ |
