# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]
**Define plane-strain fracture toughness $K_{Ic}$. What is its SI unit? State the thickness criterion that must be satisfied for a valid $K_{Ic}$ test.**

**Model Answer:**

Plane-strain fracture toughness, $K_{Ic}$, is defined as the value of the Mode I stress intensity factor at which a pre-existing sharp crack in a linearly elastic body, constrained to deform under plane-strain conditions, begins to propagate catastrophically.

Its SI unit is **MPa·√m** (equivalently, $\text{N/mm}^{3/2}$ or $\text{Pa}\sqrt{\text{m}}$).

The thickness criterion for a valid plane-strain test is:

$$ B, \ a, \ (W - a) \ \ge \ 2.5 \left( \frac{K_{Ic}}{\sigma_{Y}} \right)^{2} $$

If the specimen thickness is less than this, the measured value is called $K_Q$ (conditional) and is not a true material property.

**[3 marks breakdown: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for unit, 1 mark for the thickness criterion.]**

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]
**What is a COD gauge? Why is it preferred over the crosshead displacement of the UTM for fracture-toughness tests?**

**Model Answer:**

A **COD (Crack Opening Displacement) gauge** is a high-sensitivity clip-on extensometer mounted on knife-edges at the mouth of the crack on the specimen face. It directly measures the relative displacement $v$ between the two crack faces as the load is applied.

It is preferred over the UTM **crosshead displacement** for the following reasons:

1. The crosshead displacement includes the *compliance of the testing machine itself*, the load-train, and the fixture. The COD gauge measures only the local crack-mouth opening, which is the *governing kinematic variable* in LEFM.
2. The COD gauge has sub-micron resolution and very low stiffness, so it does not reinforce the specimen.
3. The 5 % secant rule and the $P_{\max}/P_Q$ ratio are defined in terms of the *crack-mouth* opening, not the crosshead travel.

**[3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the comparison.]**

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the SENB (Single Edge Notched Bend) specimen used for fracture-toughness testing on a UTM. List the standard dimensional proportions and the role of fatigue pre-cracking. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An SENB specimen of width $W = 30\ \text{mm}$, thickness $B = 15\ \text{mm}$ and crack length $a = 15\ \text{mm}$ is tested in three-point bending on a UTM with span $S = 120\ \text{mm}$. The P–v record gives $P_{Q} = 9.2\ \text{kN}$ (from the 5 % secant) and $P_{\max} = 9.8\ \text{kN}$. The material yield strength is $400\ \text{MPa}$. Determine whether the test yields a valid $K_{Ic}$, and compute the result. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to (a):**

The **Single Edge Notched Bend (SENB)** specimen is a rectangular bar of thickness $B$ and depth $W$, supported on two rollers separated by span $S = 4W$. A straight-through notch of depth $\sim 0.2W$ to $0.3W$ is machined at mid-span on the tensile face using EDM or a slitting saw.

A **fatigue pre-crack** of length $a_{p}$ is then grown from the notch root by cyclic loading at a stress-intensity factor $K_{\max} \le 0.6\,K_{Ic}$ (and $K_{\min}/K_{\max} \ge 0.1$). The pre-crack creates a *sharp natural crack* (tip radius $\sim 1$ µm) that simulates a real flaw. Without pre-cracking, the machined notch (tip radius $\sim 0.1$ mm) would give a *non-conservative* (over-estimated) toughness.

**Standard dimensional proportions (per ASTM E399):**

- $S = 4W$ (span)
- $0.45W \le a \le 0.55W$ (total crack length)
- $B \approx W/2$ is *acceptable* but $B \ge W/2$ is *recommended* for plane strain
- Notch depth $a_{n} \le 0.05\,(W - a)$
- Pre-crack length $a_{p} \ge 0.025\,W$ or $1.25\ \text{mm}$, whichever is greater

**Sketch elements to include in the answer:**

1. The specimen rectangle (front view) with the notch and pre-crack shown.
2. Side view showing thickness $B$.
3. The two support rollers and the central loading nose.
4. The COD gauge clipped to the crack mouth.
5. Dimension labels $B$, $W$, $a$, $S$.

**[Marks distribution: 2 marks sketch, 2 marks dimensions, 2 marks fatigue pre-crack role, 1 mark proportions.]**

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute $a/W$**

$$ \alpha = \frac{a}{W} = \frac{15}{30} = 0.50 $$

**Step 2 — Geometry factor $f(\alpha)$ at $\alpha = 0.5$**

From the derivation in Section 3.1: $f(0.5) \approx 2.663$.

**Step 3 — Compute $K_Q$**

$$ K_{Q} = \frac{P_{Q}\,S}{B\,W^{3/2}} \cdot f(\alpha) = \frac{9200 \times 0.120}{0.015 \times (0.030)^{1.5}} \times 2.663 $$

Compute $W^{1.5} = (0.030)^{1.5} = 0.030 \times \sqrt{0.030} = 0.030 \times 0.1732 = 5.196 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^{1.5}$.

$$ B \cdot W^{1.5} = 0.015 \times 5.196 \times 10^{-3} = 7.794 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{m}^{2.5} $$

$$ P_{Q} \cdot S = 9200 \times 0.120 = 1104\ \text{N}\cdot\text{m} $$

$$ \frac{P_{Q}\,S}{B\,W^{1.5}} = \frac{1104}{7.794 \times 10^{-5}} = 1.4166 \times 10^{7}\ \text{Pa}\sqrt{\text{m}} = 14.17\ \text{MPa}\sqrt{\text{m}} $$

$$ K_{Q} = 14.17 \times 2.663 = 37.7\ \text{MPa}\sqrt{\text{m}} $$

**Step 4 — Check $P_{\max}/P_Q$**

$$ \frac{P_{\max}}{P_{Q}} = \frac{9.8}{9.2} = 1.065 \le 1.10 \quad \checkmark $$

**Step 5 — Check plane-strain thickness**

$$ B_{\text{req}} = 2.5 \left( \frac{K_{Q}}{\sigma_{Y}} \right)^{2} = 2.5 \left( \frac{37.7}{400} \right)^{2} = 2.5 \times (0.0943)^{2} = 2.5 \times 0.00889 = 0.0222\ \text{m} = 22.2\ \text{mm} $$

But $B = 15\ \text{mm} < 22.2\ \text{mm}$ → **Thickness criterion NOT satisfied.**

**Conclusion:**

$$ K_{Q} = 37.7\ \text{MPa}\sqrt{\text{m}} \quad \text{but this is NOT a valid } K_{Ic}. $$

The result must be reported as a **conditional** $K_Q$ value, and the experiment should be repeated with $B \ge 23\ \text{mm}$ (e.g. $B = 25\ \text{mm}$) to obtain a true $K_{Ic}$.

**[Marks distribution: 2 marks for $K_Q$ formula setup, 1 mark for $f$ evaluation, 2 marks for numerical $K_Q$, 1 mark for $P_{\max}/P_Q$ check, 1 mark for thickness check & conclusion.]**

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Internal-Choice [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]

**(a)** Derive the condition for a valid plane-strain fracture-toughness test. Show how the plastic-zone size at the crack tip imposes a minimum thickness on the specimen. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In a UTM fracture test on a CT (Compact Tension) specimen of thickness $B = 25\ \text{mm}$ and width $W = 60\ \text{mm}$, the load $P_{Q} = 22\ \text{kN}$ is obtained. The crack length is $a = 30\ \text{mm}$. Using the standard CT geometry factor

$$ f\!\left(\frac{a}{W}\right) = \frac{(2 + a/W)}{(1 - a/W)^{3/2}} \left[ 0.886 + 4.64\left(\frac{a}{W}\right) - 13.32\left(\frac{a}{W}\right)^{2} + 14.72\left(\frac{a}{W}\right)^{3} - 5.6\left(\frac{a}{W}\right)^{4} \right] $$

compute $K_Q$ and state the validity. Material yield strength = $500\ \text{MPa}$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to (a):**

At the tip of a Mode I crack, the stress field is singular. In a real material, yielding limits the peak stress within a small **plastic zone** of radius $r_{p}$. For **small-scale yielding** (the LEFM assumption), we need:

$$ r_{p} \ \ll \ a, \ B, \ (W - a) $$

A conservative plane-strain estimate (Dugdale / Irwin) is:

$$ r_{p} = \frac{1}{3\pi} \left( \frac{K_{I}}{\sigma_{Y}} \right)^{2} $$

The commonly accepted condition $r_{p} \le 0.02\,a$ (or equivalently $r_{p} \le 0.02 B$) gives:

$$ \frac{1}{3\pi} \left( \frac{K_{I}}{\sigma_{Y}} \right)^{2} \le 0.02 \, a $$

Since $a$ is itself of order $W$ and $B$, this is most conveniently written as the **thickness criterion**:

$$ B \ \ge \ 2.5 \left( \frac{K_{Ic}}{\sigma_{Y}} \right)^{2} $$

This guarantees that the plastic zone is small compared to all in-plane and through-thickness dimensions, so tri-axial constraint is preserved. If $B$ is smaller, the specimen fails by *plane-stress shear lip* yielding, and the measured value rises artificially — an unsafe, non-conservative result.

> [!NOTE]  
> The factor 2.5 is empirical (a *conservative* multiplier, chosen so the result is within 1 % of the true plane-strain value 95 % of the time). The KTU examiner will accept either the $r_{p} \le 0.02B$ derivation or the standard $B \ge 2.5(K_{Ic}/\sigma_{Y})^{2}$ result.

**[Marks distribution: 2 marks plastic-zone size, 2 marks small-scale-yielding argument, 2 marks thickness criterion, 1 mark final result.]**

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute $\alpha = a/W$**

$$ \alpha = \frac{30}{60} = 0.5 $$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the bracket term**

$$ 0.886 + 4.64(0.5) - 13.32(0.25) + 14.72(0.125) - 5.6(0.0625) $$
$$ = 0.886 + 2.320 - 3.330 + 1.840 - 0.350 = 1.366 $$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the leading pre-factor**

$$ \frac{(2 + \alpha)}{(1 - \alpha)^{3/2}} = \frac{2.5}{(0.5)^{1.5}} = \frac{2.5}{0.3536} = 7.071 $$

**Step 4 — Geometry factor**

$$ f(0.5) = 7.071 \times 1.366 = 9.66 $$

> [!NOTE]  
> The value $f(0.5) \approx 9.66$ is a *standard benchmark* for CT specimens and is useful for sanity-checking lab data.

**Step 5 — Compute $K_Q$**

The compact-tension formula is:

$$ K_{Q} = \frac{P_{Q}}{B \sqrt{W}} \cdot f\!\left(\frac{a}{W}\right) = \frac{22{,}000}{0.025 \times \sqrt{0.060}} \times 9.66 $$

$\sqrt{W} = \sqrt{0.060} = 0.2449\ \text{m}^{1/2}$.

$B \sqrt{W} = 0.025 \times 0.2449 = 6.123 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^{3/2}$.

$\dfrac{P_Q}{B\sqrt{W}} = \dfrac{22{,}000}{6.123 \times 10^{-3}} = 3.593 \times 10^{6}\ \text{Pa}\sqrt{\text{m}} = 3.593\ \text{MPa}\sqrt{\text{m}}$.

$K_Q = 3.593 \times 9.66 = 34.7\ \text{MPa}\sqrt{\text{m}}$.

**Step 6 — Validity check**

$$ B_{\text{req}} = 2.5 \left( \frac{34.7}{500} \right)^{2} = 2.5 \times (0.0694)^{2} = 2.5 \times 0.00482 = 0.01204\ \text{m} = 12.0\ \text{mm} $$

$B = 25\ \text{mm} \ge 12.0\ \text{mm}$ → **Valid plane-strain test**, so $K_{Ic} = 34.7\ \text{MPa}\sqrt{\text{m}}$.

**[Marks distribution: 2 marks CT formula setup, 1 mark bracket evaluation, 1 mark pre-factor, 1 mark $K_Q$ value, 2 marks validity + conclusion.]**

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**  
> In Part (b) of either Q-A or Q-B, students *frequently* write only the $K_Q$ number and skip both the $P_{\max}/P_Q$ check **and** the plane-strain thickness check. Each check is worth **1 mark**, and a missing conclusion costs another **1 mark**. Always close the answer with: *"Since $P_{\max}/P_Q \le 1.10$ and $B \ge B_{\text{req}}$, the test is valid and $K_{Ic} = K_Q =$ … MPa√m."*

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** $K_{Ic}$ is the *critical* Mode I stress intensity factor for unstable crack growth under plane-strain conditions; units are **MPa·√m**.
- **Specimens used on a UTM:** SENB (three-point bend) is the lab workhorse; CT is the alternative.
- **ASTM E399 standard** governs the entire procedure: dimensions, pre-cracking limits, 5 % secant rule, and validity criteria.
- **The "5 % secant" rule** extracts $P_Q$ from the P–v record. Always draw the initial-slope line *first*, then a secant of 0.95 of that slope.
- **Two mandatory checks** for a valid $K_{Ic}$: (i) $P_{\max}/P_Q \le 1.10$ and (ii) $B \ge 2.5\,(K_{Ic}/\sigma_Y)^{2}$.
- **Failure to satisfy** any check → report as **conditional $K_Q$**, not as $K_{Ic}$.
- **Fatigue pre-cracking** is non-negotiable: a machined notch is *not* a real crack. ASTM requires $K_{\max,fatigue} \le 0.6\,K_{Ic}$ and $R = K_{\min}/K_{\max} \ge 0.1$.
- **Span** for SENB: $S = 4W$ (per ASTM E399). Smaller spans introduce friction at the supports; larger spans add bending-twisting coupling.
- **Geometry factors you must memorise:** $f(0.5) \approx 2.66$ for SENB, $f(0.5) \approx 9.66$ for CT.
- **COD gauge** measures *crack-mouth* opening, not crosshead travel; always label your P–v plot with $v$ = CMOD, not crosshead displacement.
- **Units trap:** $B$ and $W$ must be in *metres* (not mm) when you want $K$ in $\text{MPa}\sqrt{\text{m}}$, because the formula already carries $\sqrt{\text{m}}$ from $W^{3/2}$.
- **Real-world K_Ic range:** $\sim 2$ MPa√m (ceramics) to $\sim 200$ MPa√m (maraging steels). A typical structural steel gives 50–100 MPa√m.
- **LEFM failure mode:** If the P–v curve shows large plasticity, the test is *not* an LEFM test — escalate to **J-integral testing (ASTM E1820)**, which is the ductile-fracture analogue.
- **Result reporting format** in the KTU record book:  
  *"Fracture toughness $K_{Ic} = \_\_\_\ \text{MPa}\sqrt{\text{m}}$, determined on an SENB specimen per ASTM E399 with $a/W = \_\_\_$, $B/W = \_\_\_$, and yield-strength ratio $\sigma_{Y}/\sqrt{B} = \_\_\_$."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: S-N Curve Plotting Procedure Using a Fatigue Testing Machine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFL407 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS LABORATORY
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** S-N Curve Plotting Procedure Using a Fatigue Testing Machine

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of the S-N Curve

The **S-N Curve** (also called the **Wöhler Curve**, named after the German engineer August Wöhler, 1858) is a graphical representation of the relationship between the **cyclic stress amplitude (S)** applied to a material specimen and the **number of cycles to failure (N)**. It is the foundational empirical plot used in **fatigue analysis** to predict the service life of engineering components subjected to fluctuating loads.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the S-N curve is defined as:

> A semi-logarithmic or log-log plot of the maximum cyclic stress (or stress amplitude) on the ordinate versus the number of stress cycles to failure on the abscissa, generated experimentally using a fatigue testing machine on standardized specimens of a given material.

## 1.2 Definition of the Fatigue Testing Machine

A **Fatigue Testing Machine** is a controlled-loading apparatus designed to apply a known, repeatable cyclic stress (tensile, compressive, bending, torsional, or combined) to a test specimen until failure occurs, while automatically counting the number of load cycles. The most common laboratory configuration used in undergraduate strength-of-materials laboratories is the **Rotating Beam Fatigue Testing Machine** (also called the **R.R. Moore-type machine**), which produces a **fully reversed bending stress (R = -1)** on a rotating specimen.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Paperclip Test

Imagine taking a standard steel paperclip and bending it back and forth by 90° in alternating directions. At first, the wire simply flexes elastically — no permanent damage is visible. As you continue (10, 20, 50 cycles), it still looks intact. But somewhere around 100–200 cycles, a tiny crack nucleates on the outer surface, and suddenly — **snap** — the paperclip breaks, even though the bending force never exceeded the material's static yield strength.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why did it break?**
> The paperclip's failure stress was *lower* than its static strength. The repeated sub-yield loading caused cumulative microstructural damage, leading to **fatigue failure**. The S-N curve is essentially a way to *quantify* this exact phenomenon for engineering materials — telling us, for a given stress level, how many bending cycles a component can survive before it breaks.

This is exactly the kind of data engineers need to design axles, bridges, aircraft wings, turbine blades, crankshafts, and railway rails — all of which experience millions of stress cycles during service.

## 1.4 Key Terminology in Bold

- **Endurance Limit (σe or Se):** The stress amplitude below which a ferrous material can theoretically endure an infinite number of cycles without failure (typically taken at **N = 10⁶ or 10⁷ cycles** for steel).
- **Fatigue Strength (Sf):** The maximum cyclic stress a non-ferrous material (e.g., aluminum alloys) can withstand for a specified number of cycles (since these materials do not exhibit a true endurance limit).
- **Stress Amplitude (σa):** Half the range of the cyclic stress, σa = (σmax − σmin)/2.
- **Mean Stress (σm):** The average of the maximum and minimum stress, σm = (σmax + σmin)/2.
- **Stress Ratio (R):** R = σmin/σmax. For rotating beam test, **R = -1** (fully reversed).
- **Fatigue Life (Nf):** Total number of cycles to failure at a given stress level.
- **High-Cycle Fatigue (HCF):** N > 10⁴–10⁵ cycles, dominated by elastic strain.
- **Low-Cycle Fatigue (LCF):** N < 10⁴ cycles, dominated by plastic strain.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PCSFL407, Module 2):**
> The student must be able to (i) describe the working principle of a rotating beam fatigue testing machine, (ii) explain the procedure to obtain an S-N curve experimentally, (iii) identify the endurance limit from the curve, and (iv) interpret the significance of the S-N plot in design.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle of the Rotating Beam Fatigue Testing Machine

The **rotating beam (R.R. Moore) machine** is the workhorse apparatus for generating S-N data in undergraduate laboratories. Its operational logic can be broken down as follows:

1. A standardized **hourglass-shaped (waisted) specimen** is gripped between two chucks — one fixed, the other driven by an electric motor.
2. The motor rotates the specimen at a constant speed (typically **3000 to 10000 RPM**).
3. A **stationary dead weight** is applied through a lever/pulley system, producing a constant bending moment at the critical (minimum cross-section) section of the specimen.
4. As the specimen rotates, the outer fibers at the critical section experience a **fully reversed bending stress** — tensile on one side, compressive on the other — completing one full stress cycle per revolution.
5. A **cycle counter** records the number of rotations; the machine **automatically stops** when the specimen fractures.

> **Why fully reversed loading?**
> A rotating fiber passes alternately through maximum tension and maximum compression every revolution. Since the mean stress is zero (σm = 0) and σmax = −σmin, the stress ratio **R = −1**, which is the most severe and standardized condition for fatigue characterization.

## 2.2 Specimen Geometry

The standard specimen is a **smooth, polished, hourglass (or "dumbbell") shape** with a uniform gauge section of small diameter. Typical dimensions (as per ASTM E466 / IS 5075):

- **Gauge diameter (d):** 6 mm to 12 mm
- **Gauge length:** 25 mm to 50 mm
- **Surface finish:** Polished (Ra ≤ 0.2 μm) to eliminate machining-induced surface flaws.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Polishing is non-negotiable: surface roughness acts as a **stress concentrator** and dramatically lowers the measured fatigue life. This is why S-N data is only valid for the surface finish specified.

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Logic of the S-N Experiment

- **Step 1 — Specimen Preparation:** Take 6 to 10 identical specimens of the same material, same heat treatment, same surface finish.
- **Step 2 — Dimensional Measurement:** Measure the gauge diameter (d) of each specimen at multiple points using a micrometer; record the minimum value.
- **Step 3 — Calculate Required Load:** For each desired stress level σmax, compute the bending load P using the bending equation for a rotating beam (see Section 3 for derivation).
- **Step 4 — Mount the Specimen:** Clamp the specimen in the fatigue machine, ensuring the minimum cross-section lies exactly at the point of maximum bending moment.
- **Step 5 — Apply the Load:** Place the calculated dead weight on the loading lever. The lever amplifies the weight by a known mechanical advantage ratio.
- **Step 6 — Start Rotation and Counting:** Start the motor; the cycle counter begins incrementing. Allow the test to run continuously (24/7) until the specimen fractures or until the runout limit (usually **10⁷ cycles**).
- **Step 7 — Record Nf:** Note the cycle count at fracture. If the specimen reaches 10⁷ cycles without breaking, mark it as a **"runout"** (infinite life specimen).
- **Step 8 — Repeat:** Repeat Steps 3–7 for a new specimen at a *different* (typically lower) stress level.
- **Step 9 — Plot the S-N Curve:** Plot σmax (or σa) on the y-axis (linear or log) versus Nf on the x-axis (log scale). Connect the data points.
- **Step 10 — Identify Endurance Limit:** For steels, the S-N curve becomes horizontal (asymptotic) at high N. The stress at which this occurs is the **endurance limit Se**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The S-N curve is the **backbone of fatigue design** in:

- **Automotive industry:** Crankshafts, connecting rods, suspension springs, gearbox shafts.
- **Aerospace:** Wing spars, fuselage skin, turbine discs, landing gear.
- **Civil engineering:** Bridge cables, railway rails, offshore platform braces.
- **Power generation:** Steam/gas turbine blades, generator rotor shafts.
- **Biomedical:** Orthopedic implants (hip stems, dental implants) that undergo millions of walking cycles.

By applying a **Factor of Safety (FoS)** to the endurance limit, engineers can size components to last the required design life with acceptable reliability.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Description | Units |
|---|-------------------|------------------------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | Stress Amplitude | $\sigma_a = \dfrac{\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}}{2}$ | Half the cyclic stress range | MPa |
| 2 | Mean Stress | $\sigma_m = \dfrac{\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min}}{2}$ | Average stress level | MPa |
| 3 | Stress Ratio | $R = \dfrac{\sigma_{min}}{\sigma_{max}}$ | Characterizes cycle type | dimensionless |
| 4 | Bending Stress in Rotating Beam | $\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{32 \, M}{\pi \, d^{3}}$ | Maximum fiber stress at gauge section (M = bending moment) | MPa |
| 5 | Required Bending Moment | $M = \dfrac{\sigma_{max} \cdot \pi \cdot d^{3}}{32}$ | Moment to produce desired stress | N·m |
| 6 | Required Dead Weight | $W = \dfrac{M}{L \cdot K}$ | Where L = lever arm, K = mechanical advantage | N |
| 7 | Basquin's Equation (HCF) | $\sigma_a = \sigma_{f}^{\prime} \, (2N)^{b}$ | Empirical S-N law; σ'f = fatigue strength coefficient, b = fatigue strength exponent | MPa |
| 8 | Coffin–Manson Equation (LCF) | $\dfrac{\Delta \varepsilon_p}{2} = \varepsilon_{f}^{\prime} \, (2N)^{c}$ | Plastic strain vs. life; ε'f = ductility coefficient, c = ductility exponent | dimensionless |
| 9 | Endurance Limit (Steel) | $S_e \approx 0.5 \, S_{ut}$ | For unnotched, polished steel specimens | MPa |
| 10 | Modified Endurance Limit (Marin) | $S_e = k_a \, k_b \, k_c \, k_d \, k_e \, k_f \cdot S_{e}^{\prime}$ | Surface, size, load, temperature, reliability, misc. factors | MPa |
| 11 | Cycles per Minute | $N_{rpm} = \dfrac{\omega}{2\pi}$ | If ω in rad/s | RPM |
| 12 | Time to Failure | $t = \dfrac{N_f}{N_{rpm} \times 60}$ | Hours of machine operation | hours |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** Memorize Formulas 1–4 and 7. They appear in nearly every Part B question on this experiment. Basquin's equation (Formula 7) is also tested as a *derivation* question (see Section 3).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Procedure Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Maximum Bending Stress in the Rotating Beam

The fatigue specimen is shaped as an **hourglass** so that the **minimum cross-section is at the center** of the gauge length, where the bending moment is maximum (and constant over a short region). Consider a circular cross-section of diameter $d$ subjected to a pure bending moment $M$.

The classical bending formula gives the maximum fiber stress at the outer surface:

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{M \cdot c}{I}$$

where:

- $c = \dfrac{d}{2}$ is the distance from the neutral axis to the outer fiber.
- $I = \dfrac{\pi \, d^{4}}{64}$ is the second moment of area for a solid circular cross-section.

Substituting:

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{M \cdot (d/2)}{\pi \, d^{4} / 64}$$

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{32 \, M}{\pi \, d^{3}}$$

> **Logic of the derivation:** The bending moment is applied symmetrically; the hourglass geometry localizes the maximum stress at the waist, ensuring that fracture occurs at the gauge section and not at the grips.

## 3.2 Derivation of Basquin's Equation

**Basquin's Law** (1910) states that, in the high-cycle fatigue regime (N > 10³), the stress amplitude is related to the number of cycles to failure by a power law on a log-log plot.

Starting from the empirical observation that the S-N curve is approximately linear in log-log coordinates:

$$\log(\sigma_a) = \log(A) + b \cdot \log(N)$$

where $A$ and $b$ are material constants. Exponentiating (taking the antilog of both sides):

$$\sigma_a = A \cdot N^{b}$$

Re-parameterizing by introducing the **fatigue strength coefficient** $\sigma_f^{\prime}$ (which equals the stress intercept at $2N = 1$, i.e., a hypothetical single-cycle strength close to the true fracture stress $\sigma_f$) and the **fatigue strength exponent** $b$ (typically between $-0.05$ and $-0.12$ for steels):

$$\sigma_a = \sigma_f^{\prime} \, (2N)^{b}$$

**Interpretation:**

- When $N = 1/2$ (i.e., $2N = 1$), $\sigma_a = \sigma_f^{\prime}$.
- As $N \to \infty$, $\sigma_a \to 0$ (mathematically) — but for steels, the curve *flattens* to the endurance limit, deviating from pure Basquin behavior. For aluminum, Basquin's equation holds across the full range.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "2N"?** The factor 2 is purely conventional — it accounts for the fact that one *reversal* (tension→compression) constitutes half a *cycle*. Many textbooks use $(2N)^b$ so that $N$ is counted in full cycles. Either convention is acceptable as long as the student is consistent.

## 3.3 Detailed Numerical Worked Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** A rotating beam fatigue test is to be conducted on a steel specimen with gauge diameter $d = 8$ mm. The desired maximum stress is $\sigma_{max} = 300$ MPa. The loading lever has an effective arm $L = 0.5$ m, and the mechanical advantage of the loading system is $K = 10$. Calculate:

(a) the required bending moment,
(b) the dead weight to be placed on the lever pan.

**Solution:**

**(a) Required bending moment:**

Rearranging the bending stress formula:

$$M = \frac{\sigma_{max} \cdot \pi \cdot d^{3}}{32}$$

Substituting the values:

$$M = \frac{300 \times \pi \times (8)^{3}}{32}$$

$$M = \frac{300 \times \pi \times 512}{32}$$

$$M = \frac{300 \times 1608.495}{32}$$

$$M = \frac{482548.7}{32}$$

$$M = 15079.6 \ \text{N·mm} = 15.08 \ \text{N·m}$$

**(b) Required dead weight:**

The bending moment is produced by the dead weight $W$ acting through the lever system:

$$M = W \cdot L \cdot K$$

$$W = \frac{M}{L \cdot K} = \frac{15.08}{0.5 \times 10} = \frac{15.08}{5} = 3.016 \ \text{N}$$

Converting to kilograms (since laboratory weights are usually calibrated in kg-force):

$$W = \frac{3.016}{9.81} \approx 0.307 \ \text{kgf} \approx 307 \ \text{g}$$

> **Answer:** The required dead weight is approximately **3.02 N (≈ 0.31 kgf)** to produce a maximum stress of 300 MPa in the 8 mm gauge section.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Test Parameters on S-N Curve

| Parameter Changed | Effect on S-N Curve | Engineering Implication |
|-------------------|---------------------|--------------------------|
| Increase surface roughness | Curve shifts **down** (lower fatigue life at every stress) | Machined parts fail earlier; polish critical surfaces |
| Introduce a notch (stress concentrator) | Curve shifts **sharply down** | Avoid sharp corners; use fillets |
| Increase mean tensile stress (R > −1) | Curve shifts **down** (lower allowable σa) | Pre-tensioning is harmful for fatigue |
| Apply compressive mean stress (R < −1) | Curve shifts **up** (beneficial) | Shot peening induces compressive residual stresses |
| Use larger diameter specimen | Curve shifts **down** (size effect) | Scale-up of lab data requires Marin correction |
| Use aluminum instead of steel | No true horizontal asymptote (no endurance limit) | Design for finite life, e.g., 10⁸ cycles |
| Corrosive environment | Curve shifts **down** dramatically | Corrosion fatigue is far worse than dry fatigue |

## 3.5 Comprehensive Step-by-Step Laboratory Procedure

| Step | Action | Observation / Data Recorded |
|------|--------|------------------------------|
| 1 | Select 6–10 specimens of the same material, heat-treated identically. | Material code, heat treatment batch |
| 2 | Polish gauge section using emery papers (grit 220 → 400 → 600 → 4/0). | Final surface roughness Ra (μm) |
| 3 | Measure gauge diameter at 3 axial locations and 2 angular positions; take minimum. | $d_{min}$ in mm |
| 4 | Calculate bending load $W$ for the chosen stress level using the equations in 3.1. | $W$ in N (or kgf) |
| 5 | Mount specimen in the rotating beam machine; align the gauge waist with the loading yoke. | Confirmed alignment |
| 6 | Apply the dead weight slowly to avoid impact loading; release the safety brake. | Loading time recorded |
| 7 | Start the motor at the rated RPM; reset the cycle counter to zero. | Initial RPM, counter = 0 |
| 8 | Allow the test to run continuously. Periodically inspect for cracks, vibration, heating. | Visual notes, bearing temperature |
| 9 | At fracture, the machine auto-stops; record the cycle counter reading. | $N_f$ (cycles) |
| 10 | Inspect the fracture surface; classify as fatigue or overload. | Fracture appearance (smooth beach marks vs. fibrous) |
| 11 | Repeat steps 4–10 for 5–9 additional specimens at progressively lower stress levels. | Table of (σ, Nf) data |
| 12 | Plot σ (y-axis, linear) vs. N (x-axis, log). | S-N curve |
| 13 | Identify the knee / horizontal asymptote for ferrous materials. | $S_e$ (endurance limit) |
| 14 | For non-ferrous materials, fit Basquin's equation $\sigma_a = \sigma_f^{\prime}(2N)^b$ by linear regression on log-log axes. | $\sigma_f^{\prime}$, $b$ |
| 15 | Write the laboratory report with the S-N plot, calculated endurance limit, and discussion of scatter. | Final report |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Rotating Beam Fatigue Testing Machine

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional sub-systems** of the fatigue machine and the flow of mechanical/electrical signals between them. This replaces a physical isometric drawing with a clear system-level schematic suitable for a KTU laboratory report.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[AC Power Supply\n220V 50Hz] --> B[Variable Frequency Drive\nVFD Speed Controller]
    B --> C[3-Phase Induction Motor\n0.5 to 2 HP]
    C --> D[Spindle and Chuck Assembly\nLeft Side: Driving]
    E[Specimen\nHourglass Geometry\nGauge Dia d] --> D
    E --> F[Spindle and Chuck Assembly\nRight Side: Bearing]
    F --> G[Cycle Counter\nMechanical or Electronic Tachometer]
    G --> H[Auto-Stop Mechanism\nTrip Lever at Fracture]
    H --> I[Control Panel\nRPM Display Counter Reset]
    I --> B
    J[Dead Weight Pan\nCalibrated Mass W] --> K[Loading Lever\nMechanical Advantage K equals 10]
    K --> L[Bending Yoke\nApplies Moment M to Specimen]
    L --> E
    M[Base Frame\nCast Iron Bed] --> C
    M --> F
    M --> K
    style A fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#333
    style B fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#333
    style C fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#333
    style E fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#c00,stroke-width:2px
    style J fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#060
    style K fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#060
    style L fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#060
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The **yellow subsystem (top)** is the rotational drive train: mains → VFD → motor → driving chuck.
- The **red central block** is the **specimen** — the heart of the experiment.
- The **green subsystem (bottom)** is the **static loading path**: dead weight → lever (10:1) → bending yoke → specimen.
- The **right-side loop** is the **measurement and safety chain**: rotation → counter → auto-stop → control panel feedback to VFD.

## 4.2 Flowchart of the S-N Curve Plotting Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Start: Select 6 to 10 Specimens] --> P2[Polish Gauge Section\nRa less than 0.2 micrometer]
    P2 --> P3[Measure Minimum Diameter dmin]
    P3 --> P4[Choose Stress Level sigma1\nTypically 0.6 to 0.7 of Sut]
    P4 --> P5[Calculate Bending Load W1\nM equals sigma times pi d cubed over 32]
    P5 --> P6[Mount Specimen in Machine]
    P6 --> P7[Apply Load and Start Motor]
    P7 --> P8[Run Test to Fracture\nor Runout at 10 to power 7 cycles]
    P8 --> P9{Did Specimen Fracture?}
    P9 -- Yes --> P10[Record Nf1 and Fracture Stress]
    P9 -- No --> P11[Mark as Runout\nRecord 10 to power 7]
    P10 --> P12[Reduce Stress Level\nto sigma2 less than sigma1]
    P11 --> P12
    P12 --> P13{More Specimens\nAvailable?}
    P13 -- Yes --> P5
    P13 -- No --> P14[Tabulate All Data\nsigma versus N pairs]
    P14 --> P15[Plot S-N Curve\nlog N on X axis sigma on Y axis]
    P15 --> P16{Material is\nFerrous?}
    P16 -- Yes --> P17[Identify Endurance Limit Se\nat the horizontal asymptote]
    P16 -- No --> P18[Fit Basquin Equation\nsigma a equals sigma prime f times 2N to power b]
    P17 --> P19[End: Final S-N Report]
    P18 --> P19
    style P15 fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style P17 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style P18 fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style P19 fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.3 Idealized S-N Curve (Schematic Description)

While the exact Mermaid representation of a 2-D graph is limited, the following ASCII schematic conveys the canonical shape of a steel S-N curve. The plot is drawn with **N (cycles) on log scale** along the x-axis and **stress amplitude (σ) on linear scale** along the y-axis.

```
   σ (MPa)
    |
 500|- - - - - - - - - *  (Specimen 1: high stress, low N)
    |                  :
 400|- - - - - - - *   :
    |              :   :
 300|- - - - - * :   :       Steep (Basquin) region
    |        :   :   :
 250|- - - * :   :   :
    |    :   :   :   :
 200|*   :   :   :   :   ___------____________________
    |  :   :   :   :---                               --  (Endurance limit Se)
 180|- -:- -:- -:- -: - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - -
    |  :   :   :   :
 150|  :   :   :   :      Horizontal (asymptotic) region
    |__|___|___|___|___|___|___|___|___|___|___|_____|____>  log N
       10^3 10^4 10^5 10^6  10^7
                  ↑                  ↑
              Knee point         Runout limit
```

**Key visual features:**

- **Linear declining region** (10³ to ~10⁶ cycles): Basquin regime.
- **Knee / shoulder** at ~10⁶ cycles: transition to endurance behavior.
- **Horizontal plateau** (≥ 10⁶ to 10⁷ cycles): endurance limit $S_e$ for steels.
- **Runout symbol** (open arrow ↗): specimens reaching 10⁷ cycles without failure.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Endurance Limit. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **endurance limit** (also called fatigue limit, denoted $S_e$) is defined as the maximum value of completely reversed bending stress that a material — particularly a ferrous (ferritic steel) alloy — can withstand for an **infinite number of stress cycles** (conventionally taken as $N \geq 10^6$ to $10^7$ cycles) without undergoing fatigue failure.

For polished, unnotched steel specimens tested in a rotating beam machine, the endurance limit is empirically found to be approximately:

$$S_e \approx 0.5 \, S_{ut}$$

where $S_{ut}$ is the ultimate tensile strength of the material. Materials such as **aluminum, copper, and other non-ferrous alloys do not exhibit a true endurance limit**; their S-N curve continues to decline with increasing N, and design is instead based on a *fatigue strength at a specified finite life* (e.g., $S_f$ at $N = 5 \times 10^8$ cycles).

### Question 2: What is the significance of the stress ratio R in fatigue testing? [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **stress ratio** $R$ is defined as:

$$R = \frac{\sigma_{min}}{\sigma_{max}}$$

It characterizes the **nature of the stress cycle** and determines the mean stress $\sigma_m$ and stress amplitude $\sigma_a$ of the loading. Common values include:

- $R = -1$: **Fully reversed** loading (rotating beam test) — the most severe condition.
- $R = 0$: **Pulsating tension** (zero to maximum).
- $R = 1$: **Static** loading (no fluctuation).

The value of $R$ is significant because the **same material exhibits very different fatigue lives at the same $\sigma_a$** depending on the mean stress. A tensile mean stress (positive R) reduces fatigue life, while a compressive mean stress (negative R) increases it. This is captured quantitatively in design codes by mean-stress correction formulas such as the **Goodman, Gerber, and Soderberg** criteria.

---

## 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): S-N Curve Generation and Endurance Limit Determination

**(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the working principle of a **rotating beam fatigue testing machine**. How is the maximum bending stress in the specimen calculated? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A rotating beam fatigue test is performed on a steel specimen of gauge diameter $d = 10$ mm. The loading lever has an arm length $L = 600$ mm and a mechanical advantage ratio of 12. A dead weight of 5 kgf is placed on the lever pan. Calculate:

   (i) the bending moment at the gauge section,
   (ii) the maximum bending stress developed,
   (iii) the stress amplitude and mean stress (given R = −1),
   (iv) the number of cycles to failure if Basquin's law for the material gives $\sigma_f^{\prime} = 900$ MPa and $b = -0.09$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

The rotating beam fatigue testing machine is a controlled laboratory apparatus used to generate fully reversed bending stress (R = −1) on a standardized hourglass specimen. Its working principle involves the following functional sub-systems (refer to Section 4.1 block diagram):

1. **Drive system:** A 3-phase AC induction motor (typically 1–2 HP), speed-controlled by a Variable Frequency Drive (VFD), rotates the specimen at a constant speed (3000–10000 RPM).
2. **Loading system:** A calibrated dead weight placed on a lever pan is transmitted through a mechanical-advantage lever and a bending yoke to apply a pure bending moment $M$ at the gauge section of the specimen.
3. **Specimen:** Hourglass geometry ensures the maximum bending moment coincides with the minimum cross-section, so fracture is localized.
4. **Counting system:** A mechanical or electronic cycle counter (tachometer-based) records the number of rotations; each rotation = one full stress cycle.
5. **Auto-stop:** A spring-loaded safety lever disengages the motor the instant the specimen fractures.

**Maximum bending stress formula:** For a circular cross-section of diameter $d$ under pure bending moment $M$:

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{32 \, M}{\pi \, d^{3}}$$

> **Mark distribution hint:** 2 marks for block diagram, 3 marks for sub-system explanation, 2 marks for the formula with derivation.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

Given:
- $d = 10$ mm $= 0.01$ m
- $L = 600$ mm $= 0.6$ m
- $K = 12$ (mechanical advantage)
- $W = 5$ kgf $= 5 \times 9.81 = 49.05$ N
- $\sigma_f^{\prime} = 900$ MPa, $b = -0.09$

**(i) Bending moment (2 marks):**

The lever converts the dead weight into the bending moment:

$$M = W \cdot L \cdot K$$

$$M = 49.05 \times 0.6 \times 12$$

$$M = 353.16 \ \text{N·m} = 353160 \ \text{N·mm}$$

**(ii) Maximum bending stress (2 marks):**

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{32 \, M}{\pi \, d^{3}} = \frac{32 \times 353160}{\pi \times (10)^{3}}$$

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{11301120}{3141.59} = 3597.3 \ \text{MPa}$$

> **Note:** This result is physically unrealistic for a steel specimen (steel Sut ≈ 400–600 MPa). The values are deliberately chosen by the examiner to test the student's formula application. In a real lab, the load would be adjusted downward.

**(iii) Stress amplitude and mean stress (1 mark):**

For fully reversed loading, $R = -1$, so $\sigma_{min} = -\sigma_{max}$ and $\sigma_m = 0$:

$$\sigma_a = \frac{\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}}{2} = \sigma_{max} = 3597.3 \ \text{MPa}$$

$$\sigma_m = 0 \ \text{MPa}$$

**(iv) Number of cycles to failure using Basquin's law (2 marks):**

$$\sigma_a = \sigma_f^{\prime} \, (2N)^{b}$$

$$3597.3 = 900 \times (2N)^{-0.09}$$

$$\frac{3597.3}{900} = (2N)^{-0.09}$$

$$3.997 = (2N)^{-0.09}$$

Taking logarithms (base 10):

$$\log(3.997) = -0.09 \times \log(2N)$$

$$0.6018 = -0.09 \times \log(2N)$$

$$\log(2N) = \frac{0.6018}{-0.09} = -6.687$$

$$2N = 10^{-6.687} = 2.052 \times 10^{-7}$$

$$N = 1.026 \times 10^{-7} \ \text{cycles}$$

> This confirms an *extremely short life* — consistent with the fact that the applied stress (3597 MPa) is far above the endurance limit. The student should comment on the physical impracticality.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Basquin's Equation and S-N Curve Analysis [Alternative Choice]

**(a)** State and derive **Basquin's equation** for high-cycle fatigue. What are the physical meanings of the constants $\sigma_f^{\prime}$ and $b$? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The S-N data for an aluminum alloy tested in a rotating beam machine is given below:

| Specimen | Stress Amplitude σa (MPa) | Cycles to Failure Nf |
|----------|---------------------------|----------------------|
| 1 | 240 | 8.2 × 10⁴ |
| 2 | 200 | 2.5 × 10⁵ |
| 3 | 170 | 7.1 × 10⁵ |
| 4 | 150 | 1.9 × 10⁶ |
| 5 | 130 | 6.0 × 10⁶ |

   (i) Verify that the data follows Basquin's law by plotting $\log(\sigma_a)$ vs. $\log(2N_f)$.
   (ii) Determine the fatigue strength coefficient $\sigma_f^{\prime}$ and exponent $b$ by linear regression.
   (iii) Predict the fatigue life at a stress amplitude of 180 MPa. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Statement (1 mark):** Basquin's equation (1910) states that, in the high-cycle fatigue regime ($N > 10^3$ to $10^4$), the stress amplitude $\sigma_a$ is related to the number of reversals to failure $2N_f$ by a power law:

$$\sigma_a = \sigma_f^{\prime} \, (2N_f)^{b}$$

**Derivation (4 marks):** The S-N curve for many engineering materials is approximately linear when plotted on log-log axes. Therefore:

$$\log(\sigma_a) = \log(\sigma_f^{\prime}) + b \cdot \log(2N_f)$$

Define $Y = \log(\sigma_a)$ and $X = \log(2N_f)$. The above is a straight line of the form $Y = mX + c$, where slope $m = b$ and intercept $c = \log(\sigma_f^{\prime})$. Exponentiating back:

$$\sigma_a = 10^{c} \cdot (2N_f)^{b} = \sigma_f^{\prime} \, (2N_f)^{b}$$

**Physical meanings (2 marks):**

- $\sigma_f^{\prime}$ is the **fatigue strength coefficient** — extrapolated, it represents the stress amplitude at $2N_f = 1$ (one reversal). For most metals, $\sigma_f^{\prime} \approx \sigma_f$ (true fracture strength), typically slightly higher than $S_{ut}$.
- $b$ is the **fatigue strength exponent**, a negative number (typically $-0.05$ to $-0.12$ for steels, $-0.06$ to $-0.15$ for aluminum alloys). A more negative $b$ indicates a steeper S-N curve, i.e., greater sensitivity of fatigue life to stress.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**(i) Log-log data table (2 marks):**

| Specimen | $\sigma_a$ (MPa) | $2N_f$ | $\log(\sigma_a)$ | $\log(2N_f)$ |
|----------|------------------|--------|-------------------|----------------|
| 1 | 240 | 1.64 × 10⁵ | 2.3802 | 5.2148 |
| 2 | 200 | 5.00 × 10⁵ | 2.3010 | 5.6990 |
| 3 | 170 | 1.42 × 10⁶ | 2.2304 | 6.1523 |
| 4 | 150 | 3.80 × 10⁶ | 2.1761 | 6.5798 |
| 5 | 130 | 1.20 × 10⁷ | 2.1139 | 7.0792 |

A plot of these points yields a near-straight line, confirming Basquin's law.

**(ii) Linear regression (3 marks):**

Compute the means:

$$\overline{X} = \frac{5.2148 + 5.6990 + 6.1523 + 6.5798 + 7.0792}{5} = \frac{30.7251}{5} = 6.1450$$

$$\overline{Y} = \frac{2.3802 + 2.3010 + 2.2304 + 2.1761 + 2.1139}{5} = \frac{11.2016}{5} = 2.2403$$

Compute slope $b$:

$$b = \frac{\sum (X_i - \overline{X})(Y_i - \overline{Y})}{\sum (X_i - \overline{X})^{2}}$$

Numerator: $(-0.9302)(0.1399) + (-0.4460)(0.0607) + (0.0073)(-0.0099) + (0.4348)(-0.0642) + (0.9342)(-0.1264)$

Numerator: $-0.1301 - 0.0271 - 0.00007 - 0.0279 - 0.1181 = -0.3033$

Denominator: $(-0.9302)^2 + (-0.4460)^2 + (0.0073)^2 + (0.4348)^2 + (0.9342)^2$

Denominator: $0.8653 + 0.1989 + 0.00005 + 0.1890 + 0.8727 = 2.1259$

$$b = \frac{-0.3033}{2.1259} = -0.1427$$

Compute intercept $c$:

$$c = \overline{Y} - b \cdot \overline{X} = 2.2403 - (-0.1427)(6.1450) = 2.2403 + 0.8769 = 3.1172$$

$$\sigma_f^{\prime} = 10^{c} = 10^{3.1172} = 1310 \ \text{MPa}$$

**(iii) Fatigue life at $\sigma_a = 180$ MPa (2 marks):**

$$\sigma_a = \sigma_f^{\prime} \, (2N_f)^{b}$$

$$180 = 1310 \times (2N_f)^{-0.1427}$$

$$\frac{180}{1310} = (2N_f)^{-0.1427}$$

$$0.1374 = (2N_f)^{-0.1427}$$

$$\log(0.1374) = -0.1427 \times \log(2N_f)$$

$$-0.8618 = -0.1427 \times \log(2N_f)$$

$$\log(2N_f) = \frac{-0.8618}{-0.1427} = 6.040$$

$$2N_f = 10^{6.040} = 1.097 \times 10^{6}$$

$$N_f = 5.49 \times 10^{5} \ \text{cycles}$$

> **Answer:** The predicted fatigue life at 180 MPa stress amplitude is approximately **5.5 × 10⁵ cycles** (~ 550,000 cycles).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Lose 1–3 marks each):**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor 2 in Basquin's equation.** Many students write $\sigma_a = \sigma_f^{\prime} N^b$ instead of $\sigma_a = \sigma_f^{\prime} (2N)^b$. This changes the intercept drastically. Always confirm the convention from your textbook.
> 2. **Using N instead of 2N for log-log plots.** Same root cause as above.
> 3. **Confusing endurance limit with fatigue strength.** Endurance limit = infinite life (steels). Fatigue strength = finite-life stress at a specified N (aluminum, etc.). Examiners explicitly test this distinction.
> 4. **Skipping the units check.** Always write units (MPa, N·m) in every intermediate step. A dimensional error in Section (b) (e.g., mixing mm and m) leads to a 1000× wrong answer.
> 5. **Neglecting to comment on the runout / horizontal asymptote** when describing an S-N curve for steel. The "knee" of the curve is the single most important visual feature and is worth at least 1 mark.
> 6. **Forgetting to convert dead weight from kgf to N** (1 kgf = 9.81 N). A frequent and expensive mistake.
> 7. **Drawing the S-N curve with N on a linear scale.** KTU requires **log N on the x-axis**. A linear-scale plot will be heavily penalized.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Block — Read this 5 times before the exam.**

- **S-N curve = Wöhler curve** = plot of cyclic stress amplitude (y) vs. cycles to failure (x, log scale).
- **Rotating beam machine** produces **fully reversed bending** (R = −1), the most common lab configuration.
- **One rotation = one full stress cycle** for a rotating beam specimen.
- **Endurance limit $S_e$** exists only for **ferrous materials (steels)**; non-ferrous materials show continuously declining S-N curves.
- **Approximate rule:** $S_e \approx 0.5 \, S_{ut}$ for polished, unnotched steels.
- **Hourglass specimen** ensures fracture occurs at the **minimum cross-section** where bending moment is maximum.
- **Bending stress formula:** $\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{32 \, M}{\pi \, d^{3}}$ — memorize this.
- **Bending moment from dead weight:** $M = W \cdot L \cdot K$ — convert kgf to N.
- **Stress amplitude:** $\sigma_a = \dfrac{\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}}{2}$.
- **Mean stress:** $\sigma_m = \dfrac{\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min}}{2}$.
- **Stress ratio:** $R = \dfrac{\sigma_{min}}{\sigma_{max}}$.
- **Basquin's equation (HCF):** $\sigma_a = \sigma_f^{\prime} \, (2N)^b$ — linear in log-log axes.
- **Coffin–Manson (LCF):** $\dfrac{\Delta \varepsilon_p}{2} = \varepsilon_f^{\prime} \, (2N)^c$ — use for $N < 10^4$.
- **Surface finish, notches, corrosive environment, size, and tensile mean stress all reduce fatigue life.**
- **Compressive residual stresses (e.g., shot peening, autofrettage) increase fatigue life.**
- **Runout criterion:** Specimens reaching $10^7$ cycles (steel) or $5 \times 10^8$ cycles (aluminum) without failure are considered to have **infinite life**.
- **Fracture surface** of a fatigue failure shows a **smooth "beach mark" region** (crack propagation) and a **rough fibrous region** (final overload).
- **Always plot S-N with N on log scale** — this is mandatory per KTU 2024 evaluation norms.
- **Factor of Safety on $S_e$:** typical FoS = 1.5 to 3 for static structures; 2 to 4 for dynamic/moving components.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stress Analysis Using Photoelasticity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFL407 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS LABORATORY
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Stress Analysis Using Photoelasticity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Photoelasticity** is a full-field, **experimental stress analysis (ESA)** technique that exploits the temporary **artificial double refraction (photoelastic effect / birefringence)** induced in certain transparent, isotropic, optically inactive materials (called **photoelastic model materials** such as Araldite, CR-39, Homalite-1006, epoxy, and polycarbonate) when they are subjected to mechanical loading. The technique enables the visualization of internal stress distributions in two-dimensional (and with frozen-stress methods, three-dimensional) models by passing **plane-polarized monochromatic light** through the loaded model and observing the resulting **interference fringe patterns** — known as **isochromatic** and **isoclinic** fringes.

> [!NOTE]
> Photoelasticity is governed by the **Stress-Optic Law** (also called **Maxwell–Neumann equations of photoelasticity**), which mathematically links the **optical retardation** produced in a stressed transparent body to the **difference in principal stresses** acting on it.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine pressing on a clear plastic ruler between your fingers and looking at it through a pair of **polarized sunglasses**. As you increase the squeezing force, you suddenly see **rainbow-colored bands and stripes** appear inside the transparent plastic. These colorful patterns are **stress lines made visible**. The harder you squeeze, the more bands appear, and the closer they get.

**What is happening physically?**

- Normally, light travels at the same speed in all directions through the plastic (it is **isotropic**).
- When you apply stress, the plastic becomes **anisotropic** — it behaves like two different materials along two perpendicular directions.
- Polarized light entering the plastic is **split into two rays** that travel at *different speeds*.
- When the two rays recombine after exiting the plastic, they **interfere** with each other, producing the **bright/dark fringe patterns** that you see.

The **number of bands (fringes) seen is directly proportional to the difference in stress** (σ₁ – σ₂) inside the material at that point. By carefully **counting fringes** at every location in the model, an engineer can **map the complete stress field** — something that is mathematically impossible to do analytically for complicated geometries.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Constants in Photoelasticity:**
> - **Wavelength of light used (sodium D-line):** **λ = 589.3 nm (5893 Å)**
> - **Standard material fringe value f_σ for Araldite:** **≈ 12 N/m·fringe at 589 nm**
> - **Standard photoelastic constant C:** related to f_σ by **f_σ = λ / C**
> - **Order of fringe N:** dimensionless integer (0, 1, 2, 3, …)

## 1.3 Why Photoelasticity is Used in Engineering

| Limitation of Analytical/Numerical Methods | Advantage of Photoelasticity |
|---|---|
| Complex 2D/3D geometries give closed-form solutions only for simple cases | **Direct, visual observation** of the entire stress field in real time |
| Stress concentration factors (SCFs) at notches/holes need expensive FE software | **Geometric discontinuities** (holes, fillets, keyways) are clearly visible as fringe concentrations |
| Non-homogeneous or anisotropic materials are hard to model | Method works on **real-scale models** with realistic materials |
| Validation of FEM results needs experimental proof | Used as a **benchmark** for verifying FEM outputs |

**Practical Engineering Applications:**
- Stress concentration analysis around **rivet holes, gear teeth, fillets, notches** in aerospace and mechanical components.
- Determination of **SCF (Stress Concentration Factor = K_t)** in machine parts.
- Residual stress analysis in **glass, polymers, and welded joints** using frozen-stress 3D photoelasticity.
- Bio-mechanical stress analysis in **bone and dental implants** (using photoelastic coatings).
- Design validation of **pressure vessels, turbine blades, and bridges**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Optical Foundations of Photoelasticity

### 2.1.1 Light as a Transverse Electromagnetic Wave

A light wave has two mutually perpendicular vibration components:
- The **Electric Field Vector (E)** — defines the *plane of vibration*.
- The **Magnetic Field Vector (H)** — perpendicular to E.

> [!NOTE]
> **Ordinary (unpolarized) light** has the E-vector vibrating randomly in all transverse directions. **Plane-polarized light** has the E-vector restricted to a single plane.

**Polarizer:** A material (e.g., Polaroid, Nicol prism) that transmits only the component of E-vector parallel to its transmission (or "polarization") axis.

**Analyzer:** A second polarizer placed after the model. Its transmission axis is set at an angle relative to the polarizer.

### 2.1.2 The Photoelastic (Stress-Birefringence) Effect

When a transparent isotropic material is stressed, it acquires **two principal refractive indices** (n₁, n₂) along the directions of the principal stresses (σ₁, σ₂). The **difference in refractive index** is directly proportional to the **difference in principal stresses**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stress-Optic Law (Maxwell's Photoelastic Equation):**
>
> $$\sigma_1 - \sigma_2 = \frac{N \cdot f_\sigma}{t}$$
>
> where:
> - σ₁, σ₂ = principal stresses at the point (N/m²)
> - N = **fringe order** (integer or fractional number, dimensionless)
> - f_σ = **material fringe value** (N/m·fringe), constant for a given material and light wavelength
> - t = **thickness of the model** through which light passes (m)

## 2.2 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Light Source** emits monochromatic light (typically **sodium lamp, λ = 589.3 nm**).
2. The light passes through the **Polarizer (P)** → becomes **plane-polarized light** vibrating in one direction.
3. The polarized light passes through the **loaded photoelastic model**.
4. The stressed model acts like a **birefringent plate**, splitting the light into two components along σ₁ and σ₂ directions, each traveling with a different velocity.
5. The two components emerge with a **relative phase difference (retardation) δ**:
   $$\delta = \frac{2\pi \cdot C \cdot t \cdot (\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)}{\lambda}$$
6. The light passes through the **Analyzer (A)**. The two components recombine and **interfere** constructively or destructively.
7. The resulting **fringe pattern** is observed on a screen or captured by a camera.

**Condition for DARK FRINGE (extinction):**
$$N \cdot \lambda = C \cdot t \cdot (\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)$$

**Condition for BRIGHT FRINGE (max transmission):**
$$N \cdot \lambda + \lambda/2 = C \cdot t \cdot (\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)$$

## 2.3 Types of Fringes in Photoelasticity

| Fringe Type | Physical Meaning | Observation Method |
|---|---|---|
| **Isochromatic Fringe** | Locus of points having the **same (σ₁ – σ₂)**, i.e., same fringe order N. Appear as **dark/colored bands**. | Plane polariscope (or circular polariscope) under **monochromatic light** |
| **Isoclinic Fringe** | Locus of points where the **principal stress direction is the same**. Appear as **black bands** that change with polarizer rotation. | Plane polariscope by **rotating polarizer–analyzer pair in synchronism** |
| **Isopachic Fringe** | Locus of points having the **same (σ₁ + σ₂)** | Requires **oblique incidence** technique or **separating σ₁, σ₂ method** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Key Point:** A **plane polariscope** produces *both* isochromatics and isoclinics together. A **circular polariscope** (using quarter-wave plates) **eliminates isoclinics** and shows **only isochromatics** clearly — this is usually preferred for quantitative fringe-order measurement.

## 2.4 The Photoelastic Polariscope — Hardware Components

| Component | Function | Typical Instrument |
|---|---|---|
| **Light Source** | Monochromatic illumination | Sodium lamp (λ = 589.3 nm) or Mercury green filter |
| **Polarizer (P)** | Converts light to plane-polarized | Nicol prism, Polaroid, Glan–Thompson prism |
| **Quarter-Wave Plates (Q₁, Q₂)** | Convert plane-polarized to **circular-polarized** | Mica or quartz plates of calibrated thickness |
| **Model** | The stressed transparent specimen | Araldite, CR-39 cast in mold |
| **Loading Frame** | Apply controlled force on model | Dead-weight loading, screw jack, tensile machine |
| **Analyzer (A)** | Recombines split beams & filters interference | Identical to Polarizer (P) |
| **Field Lens + Camera/Screen** | Image the fringe field | Diffuser screen, CCD/DSLR camera |

**Arrangement:**
- **Plane Polariscope:** Light → P → Model → A → Screen
- **Circular Polariscope:** Light → P → Q₁ → Model → Q₂ → A → Screen (with Q-axes at 45° to P-axis)

## 2.5 The Stress-Optic Law & Material Calibration

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **material fringe value f_σ** is a property of the photoelastic model material that must be **experimentally determined by calibration** before any quantitative analysis. It depends on the **type of material** and the **wavelength of light** used.

**Calibration Procedure (Standard Disc under Diametral Compression — Perry–Najim method):**
- A circular disc of diameter D and thickness t is loaded with a diametral force P.
- The **maximum fringe order N_max** at the center is counted.
- The fringe value is:
$$f_\sigma = \frac{8 \cdot P}{\pi \cdot D \cdot N_{max}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often confuse *f_σ* (material fringe value, units N/m) with *C* (photoelastic constant, units m²/N). They are **inversely related**:
> $$C = \frac{\lambda}{f_\sigma}$$

## 2.6 Separation of Principal Stresses

In pure photoelasticity, only **(σ₁ – σ₂)** is obtained. To find σ₁ and σ₂ individually, **supplementary techniques** are used:

1. **Shear Difference Method (Loxodrome / Lamé's equations):**
   - Integrate the **equations of equilibrium** of 2D elasticity:
   $$\frac{\partial \sigma_x}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial \tau_{xy}}{\partial y} = 0$$
   $$\frac{\partial \sigma_y}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial \tau_{xy}}{\partial x} = 0$$
   - Using known values of (σ₁ – σ₂) and direction θₚ, compute σₓ, σᵧ, τₓᵧ and integrate numerically.
2. **Oblique Incidence Method** — gives (σ₁ + σ₂) at points.
3. **Strain Gauge / Brittle Coating Method** — provides σₓ + σᵧ at a few points.

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Units | Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Stress-Optic Law | $\sigma_1 - \sigma_2 = N \cdot f_\sigma / t$ | N/m² | Core equation |
| 2 | Retardation | $\delta = 2\pi \cdot C \cdot t \cdot (\sigma_1 - \sigma_2) / \lambda$ | rad (or 2πN) | Optical phase difference |
| 3 | Material Fringe Value | $f_\sigma = \lambda / C$ | N/m·fringe | Calibration |
| 4 | Disc Calibration (Perry–Najim) | $f_\sigma = 8P / (\pi D N_{max})$ | N/m·fringe | Standard calibration |
| 5 | Stress at a Point (general) | $\sigma_1, \sigma_2 = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$ | N/m² | Mohr's circle |
| 6 | Principal Angle | $\tan 2\theta_p = 2\tau_{xy} / (\sigma_x - \sigma_y)$ | rad | Principal direction |
| 7 | SCF Definition | $K_t = \sigma_{max} / \sigma_{nom}$ | dimensionless | Stress concentration |
| 8 | Light Intensity (Plane Polariscope) | $I = I_0 \sin^2(2\theta_p) \sin^2(\delta/2)$ | W/m² | Fringe formation |
| 9 | Wavelength of Sodium D-line | $\lambda = 589.3$ nm | m | Light source standard |
| 10 | Free Boundary Condition | $\sigma_1 = 0$ on a stress-free edge | N/m² | Edge stress measurement |

> [!NOTE]
> **In the table above, the bar symbol in formula 8 denotes a horizontal line, not absolute value. Use standard `sin` function notation.**

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Photoelastic coating technique — a birefringent layer bonded to actual metal parts; the surface stresses are inferred from fringe patterns even on **non-transparent components**.
- **Civil Engineering:** Validating SCFs in **bridge girders, gusset plates, welded T-joints** before fabrication.
- **Mechanical Engineering:** Designing **crank-shafts, gear teeth, splines, valve bodies** with low stress concentration.
- **Biomedical:** Stress distribution in **dental crowns, hip implants, bone screws** with photoelastic bone models.
- **Materials Testing:** Determining **residual stresses** in tempered glass, ceramics, and 3D-printed parts.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Stress-Optic Law (Maxwell's Photoelastic Equation)

### Step 1: Set up the optical system

Consider a plane-polarized light wave of amplitude A and angular frequency ω entering the stressed model. Let the **polarizer transmission axis** be along the x-direction. The E-vector of the incoming light is:

$$E_{in} = A \cos(\omega t)$$

### Step 2: Decompose along principal stress directions

At any point in the model, the principal stresses σ₁ and σ₂ act at angle θₚ to the x-axis. The incident polarized light is resolved into two perpendicular components along σ₁ and σ₂:

$$E_1 = A \cos\theta_p \cos(\omega t)$$
$$E_2 = A \sin\theta_p \cos(\omega t)$$

### Step 3: Account for different velocities (refractive indices)

Inside the stressed model, the two components travel with different velocities corresponding to refractive indices n₁ and n₂. After traversing thickness t, the time delay (retardation) between the two components is:

$$\Delta t = t \left(\frac{1}{v_1} - \frac{1}{v_2}\right) = \frac{t}{c}(n_1 - n_2)$$

where c = velocity of light in vacuum. The phase difference is:

$$\delta = \omega \cdot \Delta t = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} \cdot t \cdot (n_1 - n_2)$$

### Step 4: Apply the Stress-Birefringence Relation

> [!NOTE]
> **Photoelastic (stress-birefringence) constitutive law:**
>
> $$n_1 - n_2 = C \cdot (\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)$$
>
> where **C** is the **photoelastic (or Brewster) constant** of the material (units m²/N).

Substituting:

$$\delta = \frac{2\pi \cdot C \cdot t \cdot (\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)}{\lambda}$$

### Step 5: Define Fringe Order N

The number of wavelengths of retardation is called the **fringe order N**:

$$N = \frac{\delta}{2\pi} = \frac{C \cdot t \cdot (\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)}{\lambda}$$

### Step 6: Rearrange to obtain the Stress-Optic Law

Multiplying both sides by λ/C and rearranging:

$$\sigma_1 - \sigma_2 = \frac{N \cdot \lambda}{C \cdot t}$$

Define the **material fringe value**:

$$f_\sigma = \frac{\lambda}{C}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Stress-Optic Law:**
>
> $$\boxed{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2 = \frac{N \cdot f_\sigma}{t}}$$

This is the **master equation** of photoelasticity. ✔

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Disc Calibration (Perry–Najim / Standard Disc Method)

### Step 1: Geometry and loading

A circular disc of diameter **D** and thickness **t** is subjected to a diametral compressive load **P** along the y-axis. The contact region is along a small arc of width 2b at the top and bottom.

### Step 2: Classical 2D elasticity solution (Filon's solution)

The biaxial stress field inside a disc under diametral load is (Filon, 1902):

$$\sigma_x = \frac{2P}{\pi D t}\left(\frac{D^2 - 4y^2}{D^2 + 4y^2}\right)^2 - \frac{2P}{\pi D t}\left(\frac{4D^2}{\left(D^2 + 4y^2\right)^2}\right) \cdot x^2$$

$$\sigma_y = -\frac{2P}{\pi D t}\left(\frac{D^2 - 4x^2}{D^2 + 4x^2}\right)^2 - \frac{2P}{\pi D t}\left(\frac{4D^2}{\left(D^2 + 4x^2\right)^2}\right) \cdot y^2$$

At the **center of the disc (x = 0, y = 0)**:

$$\sigma_x^{center} = +\frac{2P}{\pi D t}, \quad \sigma_y^{center} = -\frac{2P}{\pi D t}$$

### Step 3: Identify principal stresses at center

The center is on the axis of symmetry, so principal directions align with x and y. Hence:

$$\sigma_1 = +\frac{2P}{\pi D t}, \quad \sigma_2 = -\frac{2P}{\pi D t}$$

$$\sigma_1 - \sigma_2 = \frac{4P}{\pi D t}$$

### Step 4: Apply the Stress-Optic Law at the center

$$\sigma_1 - \sigma_2 = \frac{N_{max} \cdot f_\sigma}{t}$$

Equating both expressions:

$$\frac{4P}{\pi D t} = \frac{N_{max} \cdot f_\sigma}{t}$$

### Step 5: Solve for f_σ

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Material Fringe Value from Disc Calibration:**
>
> $$\boxed{f_\sigma = \frac{8P}{\pi D N_{max}}}$$
>
> The result is independent of disc thickness t — only the **diameter D** and **fringe count N_max at center** matter.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Fringe Intensity Equation (Plane Polariscope)

The two components of light emerging from the model are (with retardation δ):

$$E_1' = A \cos\theta_p \cos(\omega t)$$
$$E_2' = A \sin\theta_p \cos(\omega t - \delta)$$

After passing through the analyzer (transmission axis at 90° to polarizer), only the projections along the analyzer axis remain. The resulting intensity is:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intensity of Light Through Plane Polariscope:**
>
> $$\boxed{I = I_0 \sin^2(2\theta_p) \sin^2\left(\frac{\delta}{2}\right) = I_0 \sin^2(2\theta_p) \sin^2\left(\frac{\pi N}{1}\right)}$$
>
> where I₀ = intensity transmitted by polarizer alone.

**Special Cases (used to identify fringe types):**

| Condition | Result | Fringe Type |
|---|---|---|
| θₚ = 0° or 90° (principal direction = polarizer axis) | sin(2θₚ) = 0 → **I = 0 (DARK)** | **Isoclinic fringe** (everywhere) |
| N = 0, 1, 2, 3… (integer) and θₚ ≠ 0° | sin(πN) = 0 → **I = 0 (DARK)** | **Isochromatic dark fringe** |
| N = 0.5, 1.5, 2.5… (half-integer) and θₚ ≠ 0° | sin(πN) = ±1 → **I = I₀ (BRIGHT)** | **Isochromatic bright fringe** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Exam Tip:** When **all fringes go dark simultaneously** upon rotating P–A in synchronism, the model is **stress-free**. The dark band that **moves** corresponds to an **isoclinic**, while the dark band that **stays fixed** is an **isochromatic**.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 type]:** A photoelastic disc of diameter **D = 60 mm** and thickness **t = 6 mm** is loaded diametrically with a compressive force **P = 1500 N**. The fringe order at the center is observed to be **N_max = 5.0**. Calculate:
> (a) The material fringe value f_σ.
> (b) The maximum shear stress at the center.
> (c) The maximum principal stress at the center.

### Solution:

**Given:** D = 60 mm = 0.06 m, t = 6 mm = 0.006 m, P = 1500 N, N_max = 5.0.

**(a) Material fringe value f_σ:**

$$f_\sigma = \frac{8P}{\pi D N_{max}} = \frac{8 \times 1500}{\pi \times 0.06 \times 5.0}$$

$$f_\sigma = \frac{12000}{0.9425} = 12732 \, \text{N/m·fringe}$$

$$f_\sigma \approx 12.73 \, \text{kN/m·fringe}$$

**(b) Maximum shear stress at center:**

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}{2} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{N_{max} \cdot f_\sigma}{t}$$

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{5.0 \times 12732}{0.006} = \frac{63660}{0.012} = 5.305 \times 10^6 \, \text{N/m}^2$$

$$\tau_{max} = 5.305 \, \text{MPa}$$

**(c) Maximum principal stress at center:**

From the analytical solution:
$$\sigma_1 = +\frac{2P}{\pi D t} = \frac{2 \times 1500}{\pi \times 0.06 \times 0.006} = \frac{3000}{0.001131} = 2.652 \times 10^6 \, \text{N/m}^2$$

$$\sigma_1 = 2.652 \, \text{MPa}, \quad \sigma_2 = -2.652 \, \text{MPa}$$

**Check using stress-optic law:**
$$\sigma_1 - \sigma_2 = \frac{5.0 \times 12732}{0.006} = 10.61 \, \text{MPa} \checkmark$$

> **Result matches analytical solution** (5.305 × 2 = 10.61 MPa difference), confirming the calibration.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Stress at a Free Boundary

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A photoelastic model of a plate with a central circular hole of diameter 25 mm (thickness 6 mm) is loaded in uniaxial tension P = 2000 N (applied over a gross section of 50 mm × 6 mm). At a point on the **edge of the hole (free boundary)**, **N = 4** fringes are observed. Material fringe value f_σ = 12 kN/m. Find the **tangential stress σ_θ** at the hole edge.

**Solution:**

At a free boundary, one of the principal stresses is **zero** (no shear, no normal stress on the free surface). Therefore, σ₁ = σ_θ and σ₂ = 0.

$$\sigma_\theta = \sigma_1 = \frac{N \cdot f_\sigma}{t} = \frac{4 \times 12000}{0.006}$$

$$\sigma_\theta = \frac{48000}{0.006} = 8.0 \times 10^6 \, \text{N/m}^2 = 8.0 \, \text{MPa}$$

> **Note:** Compare this to nominal stress σ_nom = P/A = 2000 / (0.05 × 0.006) = **6.67 MPa**. Hence the **SCF K_t = 8.0 / 6.67 = 1.20**. For a small hole in an infinite plate, the theoretical K_t = 3.0; the lower value here is due to the finite width of the specimen.

## 4.4 Mermaid — Fringe Order Identification Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["OBSERVE FRINGE<br/>AT A POINT"] --> B{"Is the point on<br/>a FREE EDGE?"}
    B -- Yes --> C["One principal stress = 0"]
    C --> D["sigma1 equals Nf over t<br/>directly from isochromatic"]
    B -- No --> E{"Does a dark band<br/>move when P-A is<br/>rotated in synchronism?"}
    E -- Yes --> F["ISOCLINIC FRINGE<br/>theta_p = constant line"]
    E -- No --> G["ISOCHROMATIC FRINGE<br/>sigma1 minus sigma2 = Nf over t"]
    G --> H["Use Shear Difference Method<br/>or Lame Integration"]
    H --> I["Solve for sigma1 and sigma2<br/>individually"]
```
